proposals.title: Stochastic Task Dynamics: A Brownian Motion Model of Posture and Movement in Constrained Force Fields
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 motor control models, including Hogan's own equilibrium-point and impedance control theories, are largely deterministic. However, recent work by Hogan and colleagues on velocity commands reveals persistent, task-dependent stochasticity in human movement. This challenges the sufficiency of purely mechanical models and suggests intrinsic noise is not just additive but a fundamental feature of the control architecture, especially in tasks like cane use where force modulation is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal structure of postural and movement fluctuations in constrained mechanical tasks (e.g., static force application, slow tracking) is best modeled as a task-dependent, anisotropic Brownian process, where the diffusion tensor is shaped by the limb's mechanical impedance and the task's dynamic constraints.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new human experimental data from tasks requiring precise force maintenance against a rigid constraint (simulating cane use) and slow velocity tracking. Movement and force data will be modeled using stochastic differential equations (SDEs). Specifically, we will fit an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process to postural drift and a Wiener process with drift to movement increments, estimating the state- and task-dependent diffusion matrix. This will be compared to deterministic impedance model predictions. Simulations will use the stochastic model to generate realistic movement variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative framework for integrating stochasticity into models of motor control, moving beyond 'signal-dependent noise'. It will directly link the statistical properties of movement variability to the mechanical parameters of impedance control, offering a unified view of stability and variability. This advances the theoretical understanding of how humans achieve reliable performance with inherently noisy neuromuscular systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the stochastic process as the primary model of the control signal's consequences, rather than as peripheral noise added to a deterministic command. It explicitly connects the anisotropy of motor noise (evident in 'preferred' directions) to the task-defined mechanical constraints and impedance, a synthesis not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Task-Dependent Modulation of Apparent Limb Mechanics: The Role of Tissue Deformation and Joint Compliance in Shaping Preferred Motion Paths
proposals.motivation: Fundamental models of human motor control, including impedance control and equilibrium-point hypotheses, often assume rigid skeletal segments linked by ideal joints. This simplification fails to capture the significant mechanical energy storage and dissipation occurring in soft tissues and compliant joints, which are critical for tasks involving impact, whip-like dynamics, or interaction with flexible tools. A model incorporating these distributed compliances is necessary to explain the behavioral consequences of velocity commands and the shaping of impedances observed in constrained tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: The nervous system exploits the passive, distributed compliance of biological tissues to simplify the control of complex, dynamic tasks by allowing the limb's mechanical structure to naturally filter and shape motor commands, leading to the observed preferred motion paths and stochastic (Brownian) properties of movement.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of task-level impedance control by modeling the limb as a multi-body system with viscoelastic tissue layers and rotational joint stiffness/damping. Using a physics-based simulation (e.g., MuJoCo), we will implement a two-stage control architecture: 1) a high-level task-space controller generating force/velocity commands, and 2) a low-level model of the limb's intrinsic tissue and joint mechanics that transforms these commands into endpoint motion. We will fit model parameters to match human kinematic and force data from whip manipulation and cane-use tasks, analyzing how tissue compliance alters the mapping from command to behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified mechanical explanation for how preferred motion centers emerge and how velocity commands result in Brownian-like task dynamics. It will yield a more biologically realistic modeling paradigm for neuromechanics, directly applicable to the design of compliant prosthetics and robots that must interact safely with uncertain environments.
proposals.novelty: While compliance is often studied in isolation (e.g., joint stiffness), this proposal integrates distributed tissue deformation with task-level control theory to explain whole-limb dynamic behavior, moving beyond the rigid-link assumption pervasive in motor control models without resorting to full finite-element complexity.

proposals.title: Robust and Adaptive Impedance: Modeling Human Motor Control with Sliding Mode and Adaptive Oscillator Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Human motor control exhibits remarkable robustness and adaptability in dynamic, uncertain environments, such as when using a cane or manipulating a whip. Pre-2026 robotics has developed advanced control algorithms, like robust sliding mode control and adaptive frequency oscillators, to handle similar challenges of uncertainty and non-linear interaction. Bridging these fields can provide a formal, testable control-theoretic foundation for the impedance modulation and velocity-dependent behaviors observed in human studies.
proposals.hypothesis: The nervous system implements control strategies functionally equivalent to robust and adaptive control algorithms from robotics, where task-level impedance is shaped not just for stability but to achieve robustness to perturbations and adapt to rhythmic task dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational model of a planar reaching/manipulation task (e.g., wielding a cane) using a two-link arm with realistic biomechanics. The controller will combine a robust inner loop, using a sliding mode controller to guarantee stability despite neural signal-dependent noise (modeled as Brownian processes), and an adaptive outer loop, using adaptive frequency oscillators to learn and synchronize with the preferred velocity profiles or rhythmic dynamics of a task. Model predictions for force, impedance, and velocity profiles will be validated against existing human motion capture and force data from constrained tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified control-theoretic explanation for how robustness and adaptation are concurrently achieved in human motor control. It will generate specific, testable predictions about the coupling between force, impedance, and velocity profiles under perturbation. The resulting framework will offer new principles for designing more natural and adaptive human-robot interfaces and prosthetics.
proposals.novelty: While impedance control is established in motor neuroscience, its explicit integration with robust sliding mode theory to explain noise rejection, and with adaptive oscillators to explain rhythmic task synchronization, represents a significant synthesis beyond current equilibrium-point or minimum variance models.

proposals.title: Inverse Movement Simulation for the Design of Task-Optimal Assistive Devices: A Duality-Based Approach
proposals.motivation: Current design of assistive devices like canes or exoskeletons often relies on trial-and-error or generic biomechanical models, which may not yield optimal dynamic interaction for specific tasks. Pre-2026 work in motor control, particularly by Hogan's group, has established that humans exploit mechanical impedance and task dynamics to achieve stable, efficient movement. This proposal addresses the inverse problem: given a known, preferred movement pattern (e.g., a velocity profile or center of motion trajectory), what are the optimal mechanical properties of an assistive device that would elicit or support that movement?
proposals.hypothesis: The mechanical design parameters (e.g., inertia, damping, stiffness) of an assistive device can be uniquely derived by inverting a forward simulation of human movement constrained by task dynamics, thereby creating a formal duality between movement analysis and device synthesis.
proposals.method: We will first model the human-device-task system as a coupled dynamical system, using established methods like the equilibrium-point hypothesis and impedance control. The forward simulation will be formulated as an optimal control problem minimizing a cost function (e.g., jerk, effort) to generate preferred movement trajectories. The core technical step is to invert this formulation using a Lagrange duality framework, treating the desired movement trajectory as a constraint and solving for the device mechanical parameters that satisfy it. This will be implemented numerically using pre-2026 convex optimization toolboxes (e.g., CVX) and validated in simulation with a planar upper-limb model performing a whip-like or reaching task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, model-based methodology for designing assistive devices tailored to specific tasks and user movement preferences. It will formally link the fields of computational motor control and rehabilitation engineering, offering a quantitative alternative to heuristic design. The resulting framework could streamline the development of next-generation exoskeletons and tools that are inherently tuned to user behavior.
proposals.novelty: While forward simulation to predict movement from dynamics is standard, the explicit inversion of this process to synthesize mechanical design from a desired movement is a novel, dual perspective. It moves beyond shaping impedances to comply with tasks (Hogan et al.) to designing impedances that directly generate the task-optimal movement.

proposals.title: Shaping Impedance Fields for Adaptive Gait Rehabilitation: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Real-Time Task Modulation
proposals.motivation: Robotic rehabilitation devices often provide rigid, pre-programmed assistance, which can limit patient engagement and motor re-learning. A core challenge, informed by Hogan's work on impedance control and task dynamics, is to design systems that adapt in real-time to a patient's evolving capabilities, promoting active participation. This requires moving beyond simple trajectory tracking to modulating the mechanical interaction based on the patient's instantaneous performance and intent.
proposals.hypothesis: A closed-loop controller that shapes a robot's endpoint impedance field—modulating stiffness, damping, and preferred motion vectors in real-time—will improve gait rehabilitation outcomes by encouraging patient-driven movement exploration within safe, task-specific dynamic constraints.
proposals.method: We will develop an adaptive impedance control architecture where the robot's endpoint behavior is defined by a time-varying mechanical field. The field's 'preferred velocity' vector and stiffness/damping parameters will be updated online using an algorithm based on Lyapunov stability theory. Patient performance will be quantified via metrics of movement smoothness, force contribution, and deviation from a dynamically-defined 'tube' in state-space, integrating concepts from Hogan's work on constrained task dynamics and behavioral consequences of velocity commands. The controller will adjust the field to reduce assistance as patient contribution increases, fostering progressive challenge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal control-theoretic framework for designing adaptive, patient-cooperative rehabilitation robots that explicitly shape task dynamics. It will yield a novel algorithm for real-time impedance modulation validated in simulation for gait, contributing directly to the fields of rehabilitation robotics and human motor control. The results will demonstrate how engineering control principles can be used to instantiate therapeutic theories of motor learning.
proposals.novelty: While impedance control is established, the novel integration of real-time, performance-driven modulation of the entire impedance field (stiffness, damping, *and* preferred motion) within a stable adaptive control framework for gait rehabilitation is unexplored. This moves beyond adjusting a single parameter to dynamically sculpt the interactive mechanical environment.

proposals.title: A Metabolic Control Analysis Framework for Online Impedance Tuning in Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Robust manipulation in uncertain environments requires adaptive impedance control. However, existing online tuning methods often lack a principled way to prioritize adjustments across multiple, coupled impedance parameters, leading to inefficient or unstable adaptation. This problem mirrors the challenge in metabolic engineering of controlling complex networks, where Metabolic Control Analysis (MCA) provides a rigorous framework for identifying and modulating high-leverage parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally adapting the principles of Metabolic Control Analysis, we can develop an online impedance tuning algorithm that efficiently identifies and modulates the impedance parameters with the highest sensitivity (control coefficients) to task performance, enabling superior adaptation to unknown contact dynamics compared to gradient-based or heuristic methods.
proposals.method: We will formulate the manipulation task as a network where task performance metrics (e.g., tracking error, force error, metabolic cost proxy) are output 'fluxes' and impedance parameters (stiffness, damping) are 'enzyme activities'. Using real-time perturbations and system identification techniques inspired by MCA, we will continuously estimate the control coefficients of each impedance parameter. A supervisory controller will then prioritize adjustments to parameters with the largest magnitude coefficients, using a stability-guaranteed update law. The framework will be validated in simulation and on a robotic manipulator performing tasks with unpredictable contact transitions, such as wiping or tool use.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel bio-inspired theoretical framework for understanding sensitivity in impedance-controlled manipulation. It will yield a practical, real-time algorithm for efficient impedance adaptation that explicitly manages parameter coupling. This advances the field beyond impedance shaping for known tasks toward truly autonomous adaptation in novel environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and mathematical translation of Metabolic Control Analysis—a cornerstone of systems biology—to the domain of physical robot interaction, providing a new sensitivity-based metric for prioritizing control actions in a way not found in prior robotics literature.

proposals.title: Modulating Motor Unit Recruitment Dynamics: A Neuromechanical Model of Variable Contraction Times for Task-Specific Impedance Control
proposals.motivation: Classical models of motor unit recruitment and muscle force generation assume a fixed, size-dependent contraction time, a principle central to the Henneman size principle. However, this assumption limits our understanding of how the nervous system can rapidly adapt mechanical impedance to comply with complex task dynamics, such as those seen in whip manipulation or contact tasks with canes. Exploring neural modulation of effective contraction times could bridge the gap between high-level task goals and low-level muscle physiology, explaining how humans achieve versatile force control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that through synergistic modulation of recruitment thresholds and rate coding, the nervous system can functionally alter the ensemble contraction time of active motor units, providing a direct mechanism for the rapid, task-specific shaping of limb impedance observed in human motor control.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational neuromechanical model of a limb (e.g., arm) with Hill-type muscles populated by a pool of motor units. The model will incorporate a novel control law where descending commands modulate the gain between neural drive and the effective contraction time constant of active units, inspired by pre-2026 work on impedance control. System identification techniques, applied to simulated motion and force data from tasks like constrained reaching or virtual cane use, will test if variable contraction time models better explain empirical velocity and force profiles than fixed-time models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, physiologically plausible mechanism for the real-time modulation of mechanical impedance, directly linking Hogan's framework of impedance control to spinal motor unit physiology. It will offer new insights into the neural strategies underlying the control of tools and interaction with dynamic environments, moving beyond the assumption of fixed muscle properties.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on how the nervous system *selects* from fixed motor unit properties, this proposal investigates how it can *dynamically alter* those effective properties. This challenges a core assumption in motor control models and integrates recruitment dynamics with task-level impedance shaping in a novel way.

proposals.title: The Scaling of Limb Impedance and Torque Dynamics Across Movement Speeds in Constrained Tasks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on human motor control, including Hogan's work on impedance shaping and constrained task dynamics, has largely characterized torque profiles and endpoint stiffness in slow, quasi-static movements. However, many real-world tasks, such as using a cane or manipulating a whip, involve rapid, dynamic actions where the time course of joint torque and its relationship to impedance may fundamentally differ. Understanding this scaling is critical for modeling human motor strategies and designing robotic and rehabilitation systems that interact dynamically with the environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that as movement speed increases, the temporal structure of joint torque profiles will not scale linearly, and the modulation of endpoint impedance will shift from primarily stiffness-dominated to inertia-dominated control, reflecting a strategy to manage interaction instability.
proposals.method: We will employ a planar robotic manipulandum to constrain arm movements to a single degree of freedom while measuring endpoint force and position at high bandwidth. Participants will perform targeted movements over varying distances and instructed speeds (from slow to near-maximum). Using inverse dynamics based on a calibrated arm model, we will compute the time course of net joint torque. We will apply established perturbation techniques (e.g., force-pulse and continuous position-dependent force-field perturbations) at different phases of the movement to empirically estimate endpoint impedance (stiffness, damping, inertia) as a function of movement speed and phase. Analysis will focus on the temporal correlation between torque derivatives and impedance parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical dataset mapping the continuous evolution of torque and impedance across a wide range of movement speeds within a constrained task. It will extend the equilibrium-point and impedance-control frameworks into the dynamic regime, offering new insights into how the nervous system manages stability-speed trade-offs. The results will inform more accurate models for human-in-the-loop simulation and the design of assistive devices that adapt to user movement pace.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has measured impedance in static postures or slow movements, systematically quantifying its co-evolution with torque dynamics during fast, goal-directed motions is novel. This directly tests the generalization of the impedance-shaping principle to transient, high-velocity tasks implied by Hogan's recent work on whip manipulation and velocity-dependent motor variability.

proposals.title: System Identification of the Human Arm's Task-Dependent Dynamic Primitives During Constrained Interaction
proposals.motivation: Understanding how the nervous system controls limb mechanics is central to motor neuroscience and rehabilitation robotics. While Hogan's framework of impedance control and the concept of a preferred motion path are established, the specific, task-dependent dynamic primitives—the low-order, parametric models of force, impedance, and preferred trajectory—that the CNS assembles for real-world interactions remain poorly quantified. System identification (SysID) from engineering offers a rigorous, data-driven methodology to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the arm's dynamic response during goal-directed, constrained interaction can be accurately characterized by a small set of time-varying, linear(ized) dynamic models identified from perturbation data, and that the parameters of these models (e.g., inertia, damping, stiffness, and a force/velocity command) will co-vary systematically with task constraints and the subject's preferred motion.
proposals.method: Subjects will perform planar reaching and tracing tasks while interacting with a programmable robotic interface (e.g., a Kinarm or HapticMaster). We will apply small, stochastic mechanical perturbations (sum-of-sines or band-limited white noise) during movement. The recorded force and motion data (position, velocity) will be used to fit time-varying dynamic models (e.g., using Recursive Least Squares or an Ensemble Kalman Filter). Models will range from a simple mass-damper-spring with a forcing term to more complex structures incorporating an identified preferred trajectory or velocity command. The task-dependence of parameters will be analyzed across different kinematic constraints (e.g., a virtual channel) and dynamic environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, parametric description of the dynamic primitives underlying human motor control in interactive tasks. It will directly link high-level concepts like impedance control and preferred motion to specific, identifiable system dynamics. The resulting models can inform the design of more natural and responsive prosthetics, exoskeletons, and physical human-robot collaboration systems.
proposals.novelty: While SysID has been used in posture, this proposal applies it to characterize the *time-varying, task-modulated* dynamics of the arm *during movement* and interaction, explicitly integrating the identification of a preferred motion path or velocity command—a core concept in Hogan's recent work—as a dynamic primitive alongside impedance.

proposals.title: Mapping the Human Force-Field: A Robotic Perturbation Protocol to Deconstruct Impedance Shaping in Constrained Tasks
proposals.motivation: Hogan's seminal work established that humans shape endpoint impedance to comply with task dynamics, but the detailed spatial and temporal structure of this force field during complex, constrained movements remains poorly quantified. Pre-2026 robotic exoskeletons and manipulanda have been used to apply perturbations, but protocols are often simplistic, failing to systematically probe the multi-dimensional field. A rigorous, robotics-inspired mapping is needed to understand how impedance is tuned not just at a preferred center, but throughout the workspace of a task.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the force-field humans generate during a constrained task is not a simple, static impedance centered on a nominal trajectory, but a dynamically shaped field whose properties (stiffness, damping) vary predictably with both spatial position and movement phase to satisfy task constraints efficiently.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-precision robotic manipulandum (e.g., Kinarm) to apply structured, multi-directional force-pulse perturbations during a canonical constrained task (e.g., curved path following or whip-like manipulation simulation). Perturbations will be delivered at systematically varied spatial locations and movement phases. The resulting kinematic deviations and force responses will be recorded. Data will be analyzed using system identification techniques from robotics, specifically a time-varying, parameterized model of endpoint impedance (mass, damping, stiffness matrices) to reconstruct the evolving force field.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, quantitative map of how humans sculpt their limb's dynamic force field in real-time to interact with complex mechanical environments. It will directly test and extend Hogan's impedance control theory from a principle to a measurable, spatially distributed phenomenon. The resulting methodology will set a new standard for probing sensorimotor control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying formal robotic system identification and dense spatial sampling—common in robot dynamics modeling—to deconstruct human motor control, moving beyond single-point or averaged impedance estimates to reveal the field's complete structure during task execution.

proposals.title: Trajectory Formation and Impedance Shaping in Compliant Contact: A Test of Equilibrium-Point and Velocity-Field Hypotheses
proposals.motivation: Classical studies of human motor control, including the equilibrium-point hypothesis and velocity-field formulations, have largely been validated in free space or against rigid constraints. Real-world manipulation, however, frequently involves interaction with compliant objects (e.g., pressing a sponge, using a cane, handling food). It remains unclear how the nervous system plans and executes reaching trajectories when the task dynamics are dominated by distributed, non-rigid contact forces, a critical gap for understanding dexterity and for designing effective physical human-robot interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when interacting with a compliant environment, the CNS does not simply plan a kinematic endpoint trajectory but co-adapts a time-varying mechanical impedance profile to shape the force-displacement interaction, and that this adaptation will manifest as a systematic deviation from free-space velocity fields toward a 'preferred center' of force-motion interaction.
proposals.method: We will employ a planar robotic manipulandum (e.g., a Kinarm) to simulate virtual compliant walls and objects with programmable stiffness and damping. Human subjects will perform targeted reaching movements through these compliant fields. We will analyze endpoint kinematics (position, velocity) and interaction forces. The core analysis will compare recorded velocity fields and force patterns to predictions from (1) a pure kinematic planner, (2) an equilibrium-point controller with fixed impedance, and (3) a novel model where the commanded velocity field is dynamically modulated by the sensed force, formalized as a stochastic process akin to the Brownian processes observed in Hogan's prior work on unconstrained motion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled experimental dissociation of trajectory planning strategies in compliant contact, extending impedance control theory beyond stabilization to movement generation. It will yield a quantitative model of how force and motion commands are integrated, directly informing the design of assistive devices (like smart canes) and cooperative robots that must interact safely and intuitively with humans and deformable environments.
proposals.novelty: While impedance control is known for posture and stability, its explicit role in the online generation of movement trajectories through compliant media is unexplored. The novelty lies in testing whether the 'preferred' motion path in force space, rather than in geometric space, becomes the organizing principle for motor commands in such tasks.

proposals.title: Disentangling Planning from Biomechanics: Is Movement Smoothness a Goal or an Emergent Property?
proposals.motivation: Movement smoothness, often quantified by jerk minimization, is a widely accepted principle in motor control, frequently interpreted as a primary objective of neural planning. However, this interpretation conflates the nervous system's control strategy with the limb's inherent biomechanical and neuromuscular dynamics. A rigorous test is needed to determine if smoothness is a cause of planning or a consequence of the physical plant's impedance and task constraints, a question central to understanding the hierarchical nature of motor control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed smoothness of endpoint trajectories in constrained tasks is not a planned objective but an emergent consequence of the neuromuscular system's impedance shaping to comply with task dynamics and stability requirements.
proposals.method: We will employ a planar robotic manipulandum to impose contrasting dynamic environments (e.g., whip-like inertial vs. stiff elastic constraints) on human subjects. Using established methods from Hogan's lab, we will analyze endpoint force and motion, decomposing commands into 'preferred' velocity-direction manifolds and separating voluntary commands from involuntary, impedance-mediated responses. We will compare smoothness metrics (normalized jerk) of the actual endpoint trajectory with the smoothness of the reconstructed voluntary command signal derived from the identified impedance model and the preferred motion primitive. Key algorithms include stochastic separation of commanded vs. emergent motion and impedance identification via perturbation techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for dissecting neural planning from biomechanical execution, challenging a fundamental assumption in motor control. It will advance the theory of impedance control by demonstrating how task-level objectives (like accurate whip manipulation) are achieved through stability and interaction control, not explicit trajectory smoothing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing smoothness as an explanatory principle by attempting to falsify it, using a methodology that explicitly separates high-level command signals from low-level, physically inevitable dynamic filtering, an approach not taken by prior studies that correlate smoothness with performance.

proposals.title: Limb State as the Direct Determinant of Virtual Position: A Test of Inverted Causality in Impedance Control
proposals.motivation: In human motor control, the equilibrium-point hypothesis and its extensions posit that the nervous system specifies a virtual position (VP) via muscle activation, with limb state (position, velocity) emerging from interaction with the environment. This causal direction is foundational for models of impedance control and task dynamics. However, recent work on velocity-dependent preferences and Brownian processes in motor tasks suggests a more integrated relationship, prompting a re-examination of whether sensed limb state could directly and primarily set the VP, inverting the classical causality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during constrained, dynamic tasks, the sensed kinematic state of the limb (specifically, a weighted combination of position and velocity) directly defines the instantaneous virtual position for impedance control, rather than VP being an independent neural command that precedes motion.
proposals.method: We will conduct human experiments using a planar robotic manipulandum (similar to prior cane/whip studies) to impose complex dynamic environments. Participants will perform targeted reaching or rhythmic tasks. We will employ system identification techniques, fitting parallel cascade models to distinguish between two candidate controllers: 1) a classical VP-command model where muscle activation (estimated via EMG) drives VP, and 2) an inverted-causality model where measured limb kinematics (position and velocity) are fed through a dynamic filter to predict VP. Model fits and predictive power for endpoint force will be compared across task conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel empirical test of causality in impedance control models, potentially leading to a more parsimonious account of how kinematics and dynamics are unified in the sensorimotor loop. It could refine the theoretical foundation for designing assistive robots and rehabilitation protocols that interface more naturally with human motor physiology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has shaped impedance to comply with tasks, explicitly testing if limb state directly sets the virtual position—inverting the standard causal arrow—is a non-obvious and radical challenge to a core tenet of equilibrium-point theory.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Identification of Limb Dynamics: A Black-Box System ID Approach to Uncover Task-Specific Impedance Control
proposals.motivation: Hogan's foundational work established that humans control limb impedance to interact stably with environments. However, traditional models often assume specific, low-order impedance structures (e.g., mass-spring-damper), which may not capture the full complexity of limb dynamics, especially in tasks requiring interaction with complex mechanical loads like canes or whips. A direct, assumption-light model of limb input-output behavior is needed to better understand how the nervous system shapes mechanical properties for task execution.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying black-box system identification techniques from robotics to human movement data will reveal limb dynamics that are more complex than canonical second-order models, and the identified models will show systematic, task-dependent variations in their structure and parameters that reflect the nervous system's control strategy.
proposals.method: We will collect kinematic (motion, velocity) and kinetic (force) data from subjects performing targeted tasks, such as manipulating a cane or generating whip-like motions. The limb will be treated as a single-input, single-output (SISO) or multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) dynamical system. We will apply established black-box identification methods, specifically Prediction Error Methods (PEM) to fit discrete-time ARX or state-space models, and nonlinear extensions like Nonlinear ARX (NLARX) or Hammerstein-Wiener models. Model validation will be performed on held-out data, comparing predictive power against traditional impedance models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven framework for modeling human limb dynamics without strong a priori structural assumptions. It will yield quantitative models that can directly inform the design of robotic prosthetics and exoskeletons requiring natural interaction. Furthermore, it will offer a new lens to interpret phenomena like the 'preferred motion path' or 'behavioral consequences of velocity commands' as emergent properties of the identified underlying dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While system ID is standard in robotics, its application to directly model the intact human limb's closed-loop input-output dynamics as a black box is novel. This approach moves beyond fitting parameters to assumed impedance models to discovering the model structure itself from task data.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Impedance Control Framework for Whole-Body Balance: Organization of Task-Specific Preferred Motion Manifolds
proposals.motivation: Current models of postural control often treat balance as a reactive, low-level stabilization problem, failing to explain the anticipatory, task-dependent coordination observed in humans. Hogan's organization-execution framework, successfully applied to upper-limb tasks, posits that the nervous system organizes motor output around task-specific 'preferred' motion patterns. Scaling this framework to whole-body balance could unify principles of coordination across manipulation and posture, addressing a fundamental gap in motor control theory.
proposals.hypothesis: Whole-body balance during stance and voluntary motion is organized by the nervous system around low-dimensional, task-specific preferred motion manifolds, and execution is achieved by shaping endpoint impedances to stabilize motion within these manifolds.
proposals.method: We will conduct human experiments where subjects perform whole-body tasks (e.g., quiet stance, sway tracking, recovering balance after a perturbation while holding an object). Motion capture and force plates will record kinematics and ground reaction forces. We will identify the hypothesized low-dimensional preferred motion manifolds from the velocity profiles of unperturbed trials using principal component analysis. Subsequently, we will apply the 'Constraint-Force Nullspace' decomposition to analyze perturbed trials, testing if the restoring forces are generated primarily by impedance modulation orthogonal to the manifold, as predicted by the organization-execution framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first formal demonstration of the organization-execution framework for whole-body coordination, offering a parsimonious model that links posture and voluntary action. It will yield quantifiable descriptors of balance strategy (manifold dimension and impedance geometry) that can be used to assess aging or pathology, and inform the design of more natural balancing robots and prostheses.
proposals.novelty: While impedance control is a known concept in balance, applying Hogan's specific two-tiered organization-execution hierarchy—separating the planning of preferred motion from the impedance-based stabilization of that plan—to the high-degree-of-freedom whole-body system is novel. This moves beyond describing balance as simple ankle/hip strategy selection.

proposals.title: Optimizing Motor Learning Through Effort-Modulated Impedance: A Robotic Cane Study
proposals.motivation: In robot-assisted neurorehabilitation, a dominant paradigm is to maximize assistance to guide patients along desired kinematic paths. However, this may reduce patient effort and engagement, potentially undermining the motor learning and neural plasticity essential for recovery. This proposal challenges that paradigm by investigating how robotic assistance should be shaped to actively promote, rather than minimize, patient effort within safe task constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that robotic assistance which minimizes interaction forces only along the task's preferred motion direction—while preserving or even amplifying impedance orthogonal to it—will induce greater patient effort and lead to superior motor learning and retention compared to traditional high-assistance, low-impedance controllers.
proposals.method: We will employ a robotic cane manipulandum in a planar reaching task with a channel constraint. A novel 'Effort-Modulated Impedance Controller' will be implemented, building on Hogan's impedance control framework. It will render low impedance along the channel's tangent (the preferred direction of motion, inferred from user velocity) but high impedance normal to it. We will compare this against a standard high-assistance controller (low impedance in all directions) and a null-assistance controller (high uniform impedance). Learning will be assessed via error metrics, force production, and retention tests in healthy human subjects and a pilot cohort of stroke survivors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, model-based method for designing robotic assistance that strategically modulates effort to enhance learning. It will contribute a new control objective—'assist-as-needed in task space while promoting effort in constraint space'—to the field of rehabilitation robotics, moving beyond simple path-following.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly decoupling assistance from impedance based on the task's dynamic structure (preferred vs. constrained directions), directly linking Hogan's theories of impedance control and behavioral dynamics to the problem of optimizing effort for plasticity.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cortico-Muscular Biomarkers of Plasticity During Impedance-Shaping Robotic Therapy
proposals.motivation: Robot-assisted rehabilitation leverages principles of motor learning and neural plasticity, but current therapy protocols are often guided by coarse behavioral metrics like range of motion or force. A critical gap exists in linking the specific mechanical interactions of robotic therapy—such as the shaping of limb impedance—to direct, in-session neurophysiological signatures of plasticity. Identifying such biomarkers would enable closed-loop, physiologically-informed adaptation of robotic control, moving rehabilitation from a one-size-fits-all approach to a precision medicine paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific features of high-density electroencephalography (EEG) and electromyography (EMG) coherence, measured during robot-assisted reaching tasks, will correlate with the rate of adaptation to a velocity-dependent force field, serving as quantifiable biomarkers of short-term plasticity induced by impedance-shaping therapy.
proposals.method: We will employ a planar robotic manipulandum to impose controlled, velocity-dependent force fields on stroke survivors during center-out reaching tasks, implementing Hogan's impedance control framework. Concurrently, we will record 64-channel EEG and surface EMG from key arm muscles. The analysis will focus on time-frequency decomposition of EEG sensorimotor rhythms (mu/beta bands) and corticomuscular coherence (CMC) with EMG. We will correlate changes in CMC magnitude and EEG spectral power in the post-movement beta rebound with the participant's evolving ability to compensate for the field, quantified by metrics of kinematic error and the emergence of a stable 'preferred' movement path as per Hogan's work on behavioral consequences of velocity commands.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between a fundamental robotic therapy paradigm (impedance shaping) and objective neurophysiological markers. It will provide a methodology for real-time assessment of plasticity engagement, informing the development of adaptive controllers that titrate robotic assistance based on neural state. This bridges the gap between mechanical interaction design and the neural substrate of recovery.
proposals.novelty: While EEG/EMG has been used in motor learning studies, its application is novel in the specific context of quantifying plasticity biomarkers *during* impedance-based robotic therapy, explicitly tying Hogan's frameworks of impedance control and velocity-field adaptation to direct electrophysiological correlates.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop EEG-Driven Impedance Shaping for Task-Specific Robotic Rehabilitation
proposals.motivation: Current robotic rehabilitation often provides fixed or pre-programmed assistance, which may not optimally engage patient effort or promote neural recovery. Integrating real-time neural activity could allow robotic assistance to adapt to a patient's intent and effort, potentially enhancing motor re-learning. This aligns with Hogan's foundational work on shaping mechanical impedance to comply with task dynamics, extending it into the neural domain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop brain-machine interface (BMI), which modulates robotic endpoint impedance in real-time based on EEG-derived measures of cognitive engagement and movement intent, will improve task-specific motor performance and learning in a cane-like reaching task, compared to conventional fixed-assistance robotic therapy.
proposals.method: We will use a high-density EEG system to decode movement-related cortical potentials (MRCPs) and frontal theta power (as a proxy for effort/engagement) during a planar reaching task with a robotic manipulandum simulating cane use. In real-time, these neural features will drive a parameterized admittance controller, adapting the robot's damping and stiffness (i.e., shaping its impedance) according to a pre-defined policy. The policy will reduce robotic assistance (increase required force) as neural engagement/intent increases, enforcing the 'assist-as-needed' principle. We will test this with healthy and post-stroke participants, analyzing metrics of movement smoothness, velocity profiles, and deviation from a preferred movement path.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for merging high-level neural state estimation with low-level mechanical impedance control in rehabilitation robotics. It will provide empirical evidence on how real-time neural feedback can be used to dynamically tailor task mechanics, potentially leading to more responsive and personalized therapeutic protocols. The results will inform the design of next-generation neuro-mechanical interfaces for motor recovery.
proposals.novelty: While EEG-BMIs and impedance control are established separately, their tight integration to dynamically shape task-specific mechanical interaction based on continuous neural signals is novel. This moves beyond simple on/off BMI triggering to a continuous, graded modulation of assistive forces grounded in Hogan's impedance paradigm.

proposals.title: Reinforcement Learning of Impedance Policies for Complex, Contact-Rich Manipulation Tasks
proposals.motivation: Hogan's foundational work established that humans modulate mechanical impedance (stiffness, damping) to achieve stable, compliant interactions with dynamic environments. However, analytically deriving optimal impedance parameters for complex robotic tasks, especially those involving intermittent contact and under-actuation (e.g., whip manipulation, cane use), remains a significant challenge. This limits the dexterity and robustness of robots in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-free reinforcement learning agent can discover non-obvious, time-varying impedance policies that outperform traditional, constant-gain impedance control for complex, contact-rich manipulation tasks, leading to higher task success and energy efficiency.
proposals.method: We will train a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) agent in a high-fidelity physics simulation (e.g., MuJoCo). The agent's state observation will include task-relevant variables (e.g., end-effector pose, velocity, contact forces), and its action will be the desired impedance parameters (stiffness and damping matrices) for a Hogan-style impedance controller. The reward function will combine task success (e.g., whip tip accuracy) with penalties for high effort and instability. Training will initially focus on canonical tasks from Hogan's work (e.g., constrained reaching, whip cracking) before progressing to novel, complex scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational framework for autonomously synthesizing impedance control strategies, directly extending Hogan's theoretical principles into a data-driven paradigm. It will yield concrete impedance policies for benchmark dynamic tasks and offer new insights into the relationship between task mechanics and optimal mechanical impedance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used RL for position or force control, applying it specifically to learn *impedance parameters* as the policy output for under-actuated, dynamic tasks is novel. This bridges the gap between classical impedance control theory and modern machine learning for motor skill acquisition.

proposals.title: A Real-Time Adaptive Neuromechanical Model for Robust Effort Estimation in Assistive Device Control
proposals.motivation: In assistive and rehabilitation robotics, control based on direct biosignal proxies (e.g., EMG amplitude) fails when muscle fatigue, pain, or spasticity dissociates neural intent from mechanical output. This dissociation can render assistive devices unresponsive or unstable. A robust, personalized estimate of intended mechanical contribution is therefore critical for reliable human-robot interaction, particularly for users with sensorimotor impairments where such dissociations are common.
proposals.hypothesis: Fusing neural intent signals (EMG) with mechanical sensor data (force, motion) within a continuously calibrated, user-specific dynamic model will yield a robust 'effort' estimate that remains valid despite physiological state changes, thereby improving the performance and reliability of assistive control algorithms.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-loop estimation framework where surface EMG and mechanical signals (from force/torque sensors and IMUs) are fused using an adaptive internal model. This model, structured as a time-varying Hammerstein system or a recursive neural network, will learn the dynamic mapping from muscle activation to endpoint force/velocity. It will be updated in real-time via a recursive least-squares or gradient-descent algorithm, using the discrepancy between predicted and measured mechanics for calibration. The resulting calibrated effort signal will drive a greedy assistive controller for a cane or upper-limb exoskeleton during constrained dynamic tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal method for decoupling intended effort from variable physiological state, advancing beyond static EMG-force mappings. It will deliver a practical control framework for assistive devices that maintains efficacy during prolonged use or user state changes, directly addressing a key limitation in clinical translation of intention-based robotics.
proposals.novelty: The approach integrates Hogan's principles of impedance shaping and task-dynamic compliance with real-time model personalization, moving beyond treating biosignals as a direct proxy to actively modeling and compensating for the time-varying neuromechanical plant.

proposals.title: Decoupling Effort from Capability in Degenerative Disease: A Dual-State Estimation Framework Using Impedance and Force-Velocity Signatures
proposals.motivation: Quantifying the progression of motor degenerative diseases like Parkinson's or ALS is confounded by day-to-day fluctuations in patient effort and motivation. Current clinical metrics conflate genuine physiological decline with variable voluntary drive, leading to noisy progression estimates and suboptimal, one-size-fits-all rehabilitation protocols. A method to separate these factors in real-time is critically needed for precise assessment and personalized therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A patient's motor output during a constrained physical task can be modeled as the interaction of two latent states—a time-varying 'effort' command and a slowly varying 'capability' parameter—and these states can be jointly and reliably estimated by fusing kinetic/kinematic data with physiological biosignals.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual extended Kalman filter (EKF) architecture. The 'capability' filter will model the longitudinal degradation of task-relevant neuromuscular parameters (e.g., endpoint stiffness, damping, or force-velocity curve constants) inferred from interaction forces and motion, inspired by Hogan's impedance control framework. The parallel 'effort' filter will estimate the instantaneous voluntary command, informed by surface EMG envelopes and deviations from a 'preferred' velocity or force profile. The filters will be coupled through a biomechanical plant model of the arm-cane system, with the task dynamics providing the necessary constraints for observability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel computational model for effort-corrected, longitudinal tracking of motor capability, yielding a cleaner biomarker of disease progression; and 2) a real-time diagnostic tool to personalize rehabilitation by distinguishing between sessions requiring motivation encouragement versus those requiring capability-adapted task scaling.
proposals.novelty: While state estimation is common in motor control, the explicit decoupling of effort and capability as two interacting latent states estimated via biosignal fusion is novel. It directly extends Hogan's work on impedance and constrained task dynamics to the clinical problem of disambiguating voluntary command from plant degradation.

proposals.title: Personalizing Hand Exoskeleton Assistance via Meta-Learning from a Physics-Informed Generative Adversarial Simulation
proposals.motivation: Robotic hand exoskeletons for post-stroke rehabilitation must adapt to a patient's unique impairment and anatomy to be effective. However, training data-driven controllers like Generative Adversarial Assistance Networks (GAAN) for personalization is hindered by data scarcity and the ethical impracticality of extensive patient trials. This creates a critical barrier to deploying robust, patient-specific assistive control in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned control policy, trained on a diverse synthetic population generated by a high-fidelity, physics-informed simulator of hand biomechanics and impairment, will enable a hand exoskeleton's GAAN controller to rapidly personalize its impedance and force-field shaping to a new patient's residual motor capabilities after minimal calibration.
proposals.method: We will develop a musculoskeletal simulation of the hand (using software like OpenSim) to generate a large, biomechanically-grounded synthetic dataset of impaired reaching and grasping motions. This dataset will train a meta-learning algorithm (e.g., Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning, MAML) to initialize the parameters of a GAAN controller. The GAAN's generator (exoskeleton) and discriminator (simulated patient) will be trained in this adversarial framework within the simulation. The meta-learned policy will then be transferred to a physical exoskeleton, where personalization for a new user involves a brief calibration session to fine-tune the controller based on measured kinematics and interaction forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for data-efficient, physics-grounded personalization of assistive devices, directly addressing the clinical deployment challenge. It will demonstrate how synthetic data and meta-learning can overcome the sample inefficiency of adversarial training paradigms in rehabilitation robotics, leading to controllers that can shape impedances to comply with individual patient dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a generative, physics-based simulation of impairment as the training environment for a meta-learned GAAN controller is a novel synthesis of biomechanical modeling and machine learning for rehabilitation robotics, moving beyond generic controllers to ones that personalize from first principles.

proposals.title: Scaling the Guided Active Assist-as-Needed (GAAN) Paradigm to a Hand Exoskeleton for Fine Motor Rehabilitation
proposals.motivation: Stroke and neurological injury often impair fine hand motor control, a critical determinant of functional independence. Existing robotic hand rehabilitation often uses rigid position control, which can lead to slacking and poor motor re-learning. The Guided Active Assist-as-Needed (GAAN) paradigm, successfully demonstrated for gross motor tasks like cane use, offers a promising alternative by shaping mechanical impedance to guide movement while preserving patient volition, but its application to the complex, multi-degree-of-freedom hand remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing the GAAN paradigm on a multi-fingered hand exoskeleton will enable patients to explore a wider range of successful movement velocities and strategies during fine motor tasks, leading to improved motor recovery compared to traditional assistive controllers, by better engaging the patient's own neuromuscular control.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GAAN framework, which shapes endpoint impedance to guide movement toward a preferred velocity field, for a lightweight, underactuated hand exoskeleton. The controller will render a virtual mechanical environment (e.g., a tunable spring-damper field) that minimally assists only when the user's finger velocity deviates from a dynamically defined 'preferred' manifold for tasks like pinch or key-turning. We will test the system in a simulation study (using a musculoskeletal hand model) and a pilot human study with chronic stroke survivors, comparing GAAN to a standard high-gain position-tracking controller on metrics of movement smoothness, velocity profile variability, and user-reported effort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first demonstration of a GAAN-style controller scaled for fine motor hand rehabilitation, a significant technical and conceptual extension of the paradigm. It will yield a validated control architecture for hand exoskeletons that promotes active exploration and may establish a quantitative link between the statistics of user-generated movement velocities (e.g., Brownian-like processes) and motor recovery outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in translating the GAAN principle—previously applied to single-point, gross limb manipulation—to the coordinated, high-dexterity domain of the hand, requiring a fundamental re-conception of the 'task dynamics' and 'preferred' motion to be shaped, moving beyond endpoint control to address intrinsic finger coordination.

proposals.title: Predictive Gait Rehabilitation via Integrated Task-Space Capability and Whole-Body Stability Optimization
proposals.motivation: Current robotic gait rehabilitation often focuses on kinematic tracking or simple force fields, neglecting the patient's dynamic stability and the interaction between task-space capability and whole-body balance. This can lead to unsafe perturbations or sub-optimal challenge, hindering recovery. A unified framework that predicts and optimizes for both capability and dynamic stability is needed to ensure safe, effective, and personalized therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a patient-specific task-space capability model with a simplified whole-body dynamic model within a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework will enable the generation of gait training tasks that are simultaneously capability-matched, dynamically stable, and therapeutically challenging.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer predictive model. The first layer is a patient-specific capability model, identified from interaction forces and motion data, defining a dynamic force-velocity boundary in task space, akin to a behavioral 'preferred center'. The second layer is a simplified inverted pendulum or centroidal dynamics model to predict stability margins (e.g., extrapolated center of mass). These models will be embedded in a real-time MPC that optimizes robot-imposed task dynamics (e.g., treadmill speed, force field parameters) to keep the patient's state within their capability boundary while maximizing stability and therapeutic challenge, explicitly shaping mechanical impedance to comply with these constrained dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for gait rehabilitation that formally unifies capability assessment and dynamic stability assurance. It will yield a method to generate personalized, stability-aware therapy protocols that proactively avoid unsafe states, moving beyond reactive assistance. This contributes directly to the design of safer and more adaptive rehabilitation robots.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a task-space capability boundary (a behavioral, patient-specific construct) with a whole-body dynamic stability predictor within a single optimization is novel. Unlike prior impedance-shaping or MPC work, this explicitly uses the predicted interaction forces from the capability model to inform the stability optimization, creating a closed loop between what the patient can do and how the robot should challenge them.

proposals.title: Niche-Partitioning in the Task-Space: An Ecological Strategy for Adaptive Gait Rehabilitation
proposals.motivation: Current robotic gait rehabilitation often employs fixed assist-as-needed or error-based controllers, which can lead to slacking or over-stress specific, already-impaired neuromuscular subsystems. This limits the development of stable, efficient, and adaptable gait patterns. A principled method to dynamically allocate task demands across a patient's diverse and impaired capabilities is needed to promote robust motor recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the patient's sensorimotor system as a community of competing agents for a shared movement resource (task-space dynamics) and applying an iterative, greedy niche-finding algorithm to assign and train motor capabilities will yield more stable, efficient, and adaptable gait recovery than conventional error-minimizing controllers.
proposals.method: We will extend a task-space capability model, building on Hogan's impedance control and behavioral dynamics framework, to quantify each joint/limb segment's contribution to the overall gait task. Inspired by ecological niche theory, we will implement a greedy algorithm that, at each training iteration, identifies the most underutilized but critical capability (e.g., ankle plantarflexion control) relative to a healthy gait template. The robotic assist (using a treadmill or overground exoskeleton) will then be modulated to selectively reduce demand on over-stressed capabilities and incrementally increase demand on the targeted 'niche,' shaping impedances to comply with this partitioned task dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational motor control framework for rehabilitation, translating ecological principles into a real-time control strategy. It will contribute a validated algorithm that systematically builds coordinated movement by exploiting, rather than suppressing, the patient's inherent capability structure, potentially leading to more generalizable motor recovery.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct analogy from community ecology's resource partitioning theory to motor control, moving beyond optimization of a global cost function to a structured, iterative discovery of capability niches within the patient's own sensorimotor system.

proposals.title: Task-Space Capability Modeling and Greedy Impedance Shaping for Adaptive Gait Rehabilitation
proposals.motivation: Current robotic gait rehabilitation often imposes rigid, pre-defined assistance, which can limit patient engagement and motor learning. A fundamental challenge is to design controllers that adapt to an individual's evolving, task-specific capabilities, particularly for overground or treadmill walking where dynamics are complex and variable. This work addresses the need for a patient-centered, model-based framework that shapes assistance based on real-time assessment of a user's force and impedance in the task space.
proposals.hypothesis: A patient's lower-limb mechanical capability during gait—characterized by their preferred force-velocity relationships and endpoint impedance in the sagittal plane—can be modeled online and used to greedily shape robotic impedance to maximize task-relevant performance metrics, thereby accelerating motor recovery.
proposals.method: We will adapt the task-space capability modeling framework from upper-limb studies to the lower limbs. Using a treadmill-mounted or overground exoskeleton/end-effector robot, we will identify the patient's preferred force-velocity characteristics and endpoint impedance during stepping via periodic perturbations. A greedy strategy, inspired by previous work on shaping impedances, will iteratively adjust the robot's assistive field and damping to minimize the deviation from a healthy reference gait trajectory while staying within the patient's estimated capability boundary. The algorithm will prioritize stabilizing the most unstable or impaired task-space directions (e.g., anterior-posterior propulsion or leg swing).
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first demonstration of task-space capability modeling applied to lower-limb gait, yielding a data-driven, patient-specific model of locomotor ability. It will contribute a novel adaptive control law that greedily optimizes assistance based on this model, moving beyond fixed assistance paradigms. The work aims to establish a principled link between neuromechanical assessment and robotic intervention in gait therapy.
proposals.novelty: While capability models and greedy strategies have been explored for upper-limb point-to-point movements, their application to the cyclic, dynamic, and weight-bearing task of gait is novel. The integration of real-time impedance estimation with a task-dynamics-compliant assistance strategy specifically for locomotion represents a significant advance over existing gait controllers.

proposals.title: Enforcing Progressive Overload: An Adversarial Impedance Control Framework for Guaranteed Muscular Effort in Robotic Strength Training
proposals.motivation: Robotic systems for rehabilitation and strength training often focus on assistance or guidance, which can inadvertently reduce the necessary mechanical tension for muscle adaptation. The fundamental principle of progressive overload in strength training requires maintaining a high level of muscular effort, but current robotic controllers lack the ability to actively enforce this by dynamically opposing the user's force output to guarantee a specific effort level throughout a fatiguing task.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by implementing an adversarial controller that uses a real-time model of the user's biomechanics and fatigue state to apply precisely calibrated, opposing forces, we can guarantee a pre-defined, high level of muscular effort (e.g., 85% of MVC) across repetitions, thereby directly enforcing the progressive overload principle.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer control architecture. The high-level layer uses a real-time, adaptive model of the user's limb dynamics and a fatigue-state estimator (based on force output and movement kinematics) to compute a desired, time-varying joint impedance that ensures the target muscular effort. The low-level layer implements this as an adversarial impedance controller, shaping the robot's mechanical resistance to oppose the user's motion, inspired by Hogan's 'Shaping Impedances to Comply With Constrained Task Dynamics.' The controller will be validated in a single-joint elbow flexion task using a robotic exoskeleton, with EMG and force measurements to verify maintained effort levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel control paradigm for robotic strength training that shifts the robot's role from helper to guaranteed overload enforcer. It will provide a formal control-theoretic method to directly implement the progressive overload principle, with potential applications in sports science and clinical rehabilitation for optimizing hypertrophy and strength gains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit 'adversarial' control objective to guarantee a minimum muscular effort, contrasting with assist-as-needed or error-reduction paradigms. It integrates Hogan's impedance control framework with real-time biomechanical and fatigue modeling for a purpose opposite to traditional assistance.

proposals.title: Greedy Assist-as-Needed Control for Progressive Overload in Robotic Strength Training
proposals.motivation: Robotic assist-as-needed (AAN) paradigms have been successfully applied to rehabilitation, focusing on error minimization to promote recovery. However, athletic strength training requires the opposite principle: controlled, progressive overload to induce adaptation. Current robotic strength trainers often use rigid, pre-programmed resistance, failing to adapt to the user's real-time capacity and task execution, which limits efficacy and motivation. This work bridges Hogan's AAN framework from rehabilitation to the distinct demands of athletic performance enhancement.
proposals.hypothesis: A greedy AAN controller, which provides the minimum assistance required for a user to complete a dynamic strength task with proper form, will enable more effective progressive overload compared to fixed-resistance protocols by optimally challenging the user's time-varying force-generating capacity.
proposals.method: We will implement a greedy AAN algorithm on a high-impedance robotic actuator (e.g., a modified MIT-MANUS or cable-driven system). The controller will use real-time estimates of the user's limb endpoint force, derived from interaction torque and a model of limb dynamics, to compute the 'needed' assistance. Inspired by Hogan's 'Shaping Impedances...' work, the task dynamics (e.g., a simulated whip or constrained path) will define a velocity-dependent vector field. The controller will greedily subtract assistive forces only when the user's commanded velocity, inferred from their applied force and the limb's mechanical impedance, deviates from the task's preferred kinematic path, ensuring overload while maintaining task compliance.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a formal control-theoretic framework for robotic overload training, extending AAN principles beyond rehabilitation. It will yield a novel algorithm that dynamically titrates resistance based on real-time biomechanical performance, contributing to the fields of sports robotics and human motor learning. The results will demonstrate how robotic systems can be task partners that adaptively challenge, rather than merely assist, the human user.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is inverting the greedy AAN objective from 'minimizing error' to 'minimizing assistance,' applying it to strength training for overload rather than rehabilitation for recovery. Furthermore, it integrates this with Hogan's concepts of task-dynamic compliance and behavioral consequences of velocity commands to maintain proper movement quality under high load.

proposals.title: Task-Oriented Motor Priming via Intermittent Field-Based Contacts: A GAAN-Controlled Framework for Safe Home Telerehabilitation
proposals.motivation: Current home-based rehabilitation robots rely on continuous, rigid mechanical coupling, which poses safety risks in unsupervised environments and increases system cost and complexity. A paradigm shift toward intermittent, non-rigid interaction could enable safer, more accessible therapy. This research addresses the critical need to deliver effective, task-specific therapeutic forces without persistent physical links, leveraging pre-2026 advances in simplified robust control and understanding of human motor adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: Therapeutic motion and impedance modulation for upper-limb rehabilitation can be effectively achieved through sequences of intermittent, field-based contacts (e.g., air jets or brief tactile stimuli) orchestrated by a simplified controller that shapes the patient's limb dynamics toward a task-specific preferred movement path.
proposals.method: We will develop a control architecture based on the Goal-Attracting, Adaptive Nonlinear (GAAN) controller to command a repositionable, non-contacting actuator (e.g., an air jet array). The controller will use real-time estimates of limb state (position, velocity) to trigger brief, spatially-targeted force pulses, shaping the limb's trajectory toward a desired, therapy-relevant 'preferred' path. The interaction will be modeled as a series of impulsive perturbations, and we will analyze the resulting limb dynamics as a stochastic process, akin to prior work on Brownian processes in motor control, to quantify therapeutic dose.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control framework for safe, low-cost telerehabilitation, eliminating the need for complex, rigid exoskeletons. It will yield fundamental insights into how sequences of discrete physical interactions can be used to shape continuous human motor behavior, extending the theory of impedance control to intermittent-contact paradigms.
proposals.novelty: This approach fundamentally rethinks robotic therapy by abandoning continuous mechanical coupling, instead using simplified control to achieve therapeutic goals through stochastic, field-based mechanical interactions, a significant departure from pre-2026 continuous-assist paradigms.

proposals.title: Non-Stationary Objective Functions in Motor Control: A Study of Jerk-Weight Adaptation with Practice and Fatigue
proposals.motivation: Optimal control models of human movement often assume a stationary objective function, such as minimizing jerk or effort, to explain stable, skilled performance. However, this assumption is challenged by the dynamics of learning and fatigue, where the nervous system's priorities may shift. Understanding how performance criteria evolve is crucial for refining models of motor adaptation and for designing assistive devices, like canes or exoskeletons, that can adapt to a user's changing state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the weight assigned to smoothness (jerk) in an implicit motor objective function decreases systematically with practice as efficiency is prioritized, and increases with the onset of fatigue as stability becomes paramount.
proposals.method: We will analyze human motion capture and force data from a repetitive, constrained reaching task performed with a manipulandum. Using pre-2026 methods, we will fit time-varying Linear-Quadratic Regulator (LQR) or Iterative Linear Quadratic Gaussian (iLQG) models to the data, allowing the cost function weights (e.g., on jerk, force, and error) to be free parameters estimated in sliding windows. Changes in these weights will be correlated with independent measures of practice (trial number) and fatigue (e.g., electromyographic spectral shift, self-report).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct empirical evidence for a non-stationary objective function in human motor control, linking cost function dynamics to behavioral states. It will extend Hogan's framework of impedance control and task dynamics by showing how the 'preferred' solution is shaped by time-varying internal priorities, not just external constraints.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified changes in movement kinematics with learning/fatigue, this proposal directly tests the novel idea that the underlying optimality criterion itself is fluid, challenging a foundational assumption of computational motor control models.

proposals.title: Impedance Modulation for Task Compliance in Movements Initiated from Non-Zero Velocity
proposals.motivation: Classical motor control models, including equilibrium-point and impedance control theories, often assume movements start from rest. However, many natural and tool-mediated tasks, such as using a cane or manipulating a whip, involve initiating a new action from an ongoing state of motion. Pre-2026, the role of initial momentum and its interaction with task-specific impedance shaping for stability and accuracy remains poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that humans exploit non-zero initial conditions by modulating endpoint impedance in a task-dependent manner, not merely to dampen motion, but to channel existing momentum to comply with constrained task dynamics efficiently.
proposals.method: We will conduct human experiments where participants perform targeted reaching or cane-mediated pointing tasks starting with a pre-imposed handle velocity. Using a robotic manipulandum, we will measure force and motion to identify the time-varying endpoint stiffness and damping via established algorithms (e.g., least-squares estimation from perturbed trials). We will analyze how the 'preferred' velocity field and the shaped mechanical impedance co-vary with the initial kinetic state to achieve task goals, extending the analysis of constrained task dynamics to non-stationary starts.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the theory of impedance control to a broader class of dynamic interactions, providing a principled model of how the nervous system integrates initial momentum into motor planning. It will offer new insights into the control of tools and interactions with dynamic environments, with implications for robotics and rehabilitation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hogan and others has studied impedance in constrained tasks and the consequences of velocity commands, systematically modeling and experimentally dissecting the control strategy for movements starting from non-zero velocity—treating initial momentum as a feature, not a disturbance—is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Inferring Task-Specific Control Laws in Human Whip Manipulation via Impedance-Based System Identification
proposals.motivation: Understanding how humans control complex, underactuated objects like whips—where the dynamics are non-linear and distributed—remains a fundamental challenge in motor neuroscience. Pre-2026 work on impedance control and task dynamics provides a framework, but the specific control laws humans employ for such dynamic manipulation are not well characterized. This gap limits our ability to design effective robotic controllers and rehabilitation protocols for tasks involving flexible objects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that human control during whip manipulation can be modeled as the online shaping of endpoint impedance to comply with the whip's task-specific, time-varying dynamics, and that this control law can be identified from movement kinematics and interaction forces.
proposals.method: We will collect human movement data (kinematics and forces) during a rhythmic whip-targeting task. Using a physics-based simulation of the whip, we will employ a two-stage system identification approach from robotics. First, we will fit a parameterized, task-level dynamical system model (e.g., a non-linear oscillator with forcing terms) to the observed endpoint (hand) motion. Second, we will use an inverse dynamics algorithm to infer the time-varying endpoint impedance (stiffness and damping) profiles required to generate the identified model's behavior within the simulated whip dynamics, treating the inferred impedance as the control law.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, model-based description of the control laws underlying dynamic manipulation of flexible objects, extending Hogan's impedance control paradigm to a novel domain. It will yield a methodology to dissociate task-level dynamics from neuromuscular control strategy, offering new insights for both motor control theory and the design of robotic controllers for dynamic, underactuated systems.
proposals.novelty: While system ID is common in robotics, applying it to infer the impedance control laws humans use for a highly dynamic, non-prehensile manipulation task is novel. It moves beyond characterizing the 'preferred' movement path to explicitly identifying the time-varying mechanical interaction strategy that generates it.

proposals.title: Whole-Body Reaching as a Composition of Impedance-Primed Velocity Fields: A Model of Postural Coordination for Unconstrained Task Dynamics
