proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on human motor control, including Hogan's work, has successfully modeled arm and hand movements using impedance shaping and velocity-based primitives. However, whole-body movements, such as reaching beyond arm's length requiring torso and leg coordination, present a critical gap. Understanding how the nervous system scales these control principles to manage the complex, high-dimensional dynamics of the entire body is essential for advancing theories of motor coordination and for developing robust, whole-body robotic controllers.
proposals.hypothesis: Whole-body reaching movements are generated by the composition of simple, task-specific velocity command fields, with the mechanical impedance of the torso and legs shaped to stabilize the motion and comply with the constraints of the task, analogous to principles demonstrated in upper-limb studies.
proposals.method: We will extend the computational framework from prior whip and constrained task studies. Human motion capture data of whole-body reaching to varied targets will be analyzed to identify preferred velocity fields and centers of motion for the torso and legs. Using a multi-joint, torque-controlled robot model in simulation (e.g., extending from prior simulation studies), we will implement an algorithm that generates movement via the superposition of these velocity fields. The controller will adapt endpoint impedance using methods from 'Shaping Impedances...', treating the body as a coupled mechanical system, to achieve stable, goal-directed motion despite the increased degrees of freedom.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated model of whole-body reaching grounded in the impedance-control paradigm, directly extending Hogan's upper-limb theories. It will offer a principled explanation for the coordination of posture and movement and deliver a novel control architecture for whole-body robotic manipulation that is inherently compliant and task-aware.
proposals.novelty: While velocity fields and impedance shaping have been studied for limbs, their explicit integration and scaling to control the nonlinear, coupled dynamics of a whole-body chain for unconstrained reaching is a novel and non-trivial extension. It moves beyond analyzing the arm in isolation to a systems-level view of postural coordination.

proposals.title: Modeling Sit-to-Stand as a Stochastic Optimal Control Problem to Predict Individual Recovery Trajectories in Neurorehabilitation
proposals.motivation: The sit-to-stand (STS) transition is a critical, mechanically demanding task of daily living, and its recovery is a primary goal in neurorehabilitation (e.g., post-stroke). Current clinical assessments are often descriptive and lack a predictive, model-based framework to understand the underlying control strategies and forecast an individual's recovery path. Grounded in pre-2026 motor control theory, this gap limits personalized, theory-driven therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the evolution of STS performance during recovery can be predicted by a computational model where the nervous system solves a stochastic optimal control problem, trading off effort, stability (via impedance shaping), and task success under sensorimotor noise.
proposals.method: We will formulate STS as a finite-horizon, partially observable stochastic optimal control problem. The biomechanical plant will be a 2- or 3-segment inverted pendulum model actuated by muscles with tunable intrinsic impedance. The cost function will penalize effort, center-of-mass velocity deviation from a 'preferred' profile, and final upright instability. We will solve for the optimal feedback control law using iterative Linear Quadratic Gaussian (iLQG) methods. Model parameters (e.g., noise levels, cost weights, impedance) will be fit to kinematic and force-plate data from patients at different recovery stages to generate personalized recovery predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, model-based tool to predict individual recovery trajectories, moving beyond population averages. It will offer a mechanistic interpretation of observed motor strategies (e.g., use of momentum, force modulation) through the lens of optimality under constraints. This could inform the design of targeted assistive devices and therapy protocols.
proposals.novelty: While optimal control has been applied to normative STS, its use as a predictive model for the time-evolving, pathological recovery process is novel. Integrating Hogan's concepts of impedance control and preferred movement patterns into the optimality criterion for a clinical population represents a significant advance from prior descriptive biomechanical studies.

proposals.title: Inferring Stroke Lesion Topography from Unconstrained Reaching Kinematics: An Inverse Modeling Approach Using Stochastic Task Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Post-stroke motor deficits are highly heterogeneous, complicating prognosis and targeted therapy. Current lesion-symptom mapping relies on coarse clinical scales or constrained lab tasks, which may not capture the full spectrum of impairment in natural movement. A method to predict lesion location and extent from detailed, unconstrained movement kinematics would provide a direct, quantitative link between neural damage and emergent motor behavior, enhancing personalized assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial pattern of a stroke lesion can be predicted by inverting a computational model of the human motor control system, where the lesion is represented as a specific perturbation to the model's impedance and stochastic command parameters, and the model's output is fit to observed kinematic patterns.
proposals.method: We will record unconstrained, planar reaching movements in chronic stroke survivors using a robotic manipulandum, extracting STS (Smoothness, Temporal Structure, Spatial Span) kinematics. A computational model of the arm will be developed based on Hogan's framework of impedance control and stochastic velocity commands (Brownian process). The 'inverse problem' will be solved using a Bayesian optimization routine to iteratively adjust simulated 'lesion parameters' (modeled as localized changes to impedance and noise characteristics in a musculoskeletal model) until the simulated kinematics match the patient's recorded STS profile. The predicted lesion parameters will be validated against clinical neuroimaging (MRI).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, model-based diagnostic tool that translates complex movement patterns into specific neural substrate hypotheses. It will contribute a formal, mechanistic understanding of how focal brain damage disturbs the impedance and stochastic properties of motor control, moving beyond descriptive correlation to causal modeling of lesion-behavior relationships.
proposals.novelty: While lesion-symptom mapping is common, inverting a stochastic impedance control model to predict lesion topography from natural kinematics is a novel, causal approach. It uniquely combines Hogan's principles of mechanical impedance and velocity-command noise within an inverse problem framework for neurological diagnosis.

proposals.title: Antagonist-Dominant Torque Generation with Agonist-Mediated Stiffness Shaping: A Novel Control Paradigm for Dynamic Tasks
proposals.motivation: Traditional models of motor control assign the primary torque-generating role to agonist muscles, with antagonists providing braking or co-contraction for stiffness. This paradigm may limit performance in tasks requiring rapid, high-force reversals or precise modulation of endpoint impedance, such as wielding a whip or cane. A control scheme that fundamentally inverts these roles could offer new insights into the versatility of the neuromuscular system and inspire novel robotic control architectures for dynamic manipulation.
proposals.hypothesis: Assigning the primary torque command to the antagonist muscle group, while using the agonist for co-activation to shape endpoint stiffness, will enhance performance in tasks characterized by underdamped, non-contact dynamics and improve stability during forceful interactions with constrained environments.
proposals.method: We will employ a planar, two-degree-of-freedom human arm model with agonist-antagonist muscle pairs in simulation. A novel control law will be developed where the desired joint torque is generated primarily by the antagonist's force-length-velocity relationship. The agonist will be activated proportionally to a stiffness reference derived from the task dynamics, informed by Hogan's 'Shaping Impedances' framework. We will test this scheme in two simulated tasks: 1) a whip-manipulation task requiring precise tip control, and 2) a constrained reaching task with unexpected mechanical perturbations, analyzing metrics of task success, energy, and stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretical and computational demonstration of a non-canonical motor control strategy, expanding the understanding of how impedance and force can be decoupled and recombined. It will contribute a new control architecture for robotic systems performing dynamic tasks, where endpoint stiffness and net torque can be modulated more independently. The results will challenge conventional assumptions about muscle role assignment in both biological and engineered systems.
proposals.novelty: While co-contraction is well-studied, the explicit inversion of the primary torque and stiffness-modulation roles between agonist and antagonist pairs is a radical departure from standard models. This approach uniquely leverages the antagonist's mechanical advantage for force generation in specific dynamic contexts, a concept not explored in prior impedance control or equilibrium-point literature.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Joint Stiffness Modulation via Co-activation: Modeling and Exploitation for Robust Impedance Control in Dynamic Tasks
proposals.motivation: Human motor control excels in dynamic, unpredictable environments by modulating limb impedance, primarily through muscle co-activation. Pre-2026 models often assume linear or piecewise-linear joint stiffness, which may inadequately capture the complex, nonlinear relationship between co-activation levels and the resulting mechanical impedance. A more accurate model is crucial for developing robotic and prosthetic controllers that can match human dexterity in tasks like tool manipulation (e.g., canes, whips) where contact forces and task dynamics are highly variable.
proposals.hypothesis: Joint stiffness exhibits a nonlinear, saturating function of muscle co-activation level, and explicitly modeling this relationship will enable more stable and efficient impedance control strategies for robots performing dynamic, contact-rich tasks.
proposals.method: We will first conduct human experiments using a planar robotic manipulandum to measure endpoint stiffness (via perturbation methods) across a range of instructed co-activation levels during postural and simple movement tasks. The data will be used to identify a nonlinear stiffness model (e.g., a sigmoidal function) of co-activation. This model will then be implemented in a simulation of a torque-controlled robot arm performing two dynamic tasks: 1) a whip manipulation task (extending prior simulation work) and 2) a constrained reaching task with unexpected contact. Control will be based on the equilibrium-point hypothesis, where the nonlinear stiffness model shapes the impedance around a moving equilibrium trajectory derived from simple velocity-based primitives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, quantitative model of nonlinear stiffness modulation via co-activation, directly informing neuromechanical control theory. It will also demonstrate a novel impedance control architecture for robots that leverages this model to achieve more human-like robustness and compliance in dynamic tasks, advancing the field of physical human-robot interaction and rehabilitation robotics.
proposals.novelty: While co-activation's role in stiffness modulation is known, explicitly modeling its nonlinearity and integrating it into a task-level impedance control framework for dynamic manipulation is novel. This moves beyond the linear approximations common in pre-2026 robot control and the descriptive models of human motor control.

proposals.title: Antagonistic Co-activation for Multi-Joint Stability: A Model-Based FES Control Strategy for Reaching and Gait
proposals.motivation: Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) systems for restoring movement in individuals with neurological impairment often struggle with poor stability and high metabolic cost due to the difficulty of coordinating multiple muscles across joints. Pre-2026 work by Hogan and others established that antagonistic co-activation is a fundamental biological strategy for shaping endpoint impedance and ensuring stability in single-joint tasks. Extending this principle to multi-joint, dynamic tasks like walking or reaching could address a critical limitation in neuroprosthetics.
proposals.hypothesis: A model-based FES controller that explicitly regulates antagonistic muscle co-activation patterns, derived from the task's constrained dynamics and stability requirements, will produce more stable, accurate, and metabolically efficient multi-joint movements compared to traditional trajectory-tracking controllers.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical control architecture. First, a high-level task planner, informed by the 'shaping impedances to comply with constrained task dynamics' framework, will define the required endpoint stiffness and damping for a given phase of movement (e.g., single-leg stance in gait). Second, a mid-level controller will solve the muscle redundancy problem using a static optimization algorithm to map the desired multi-joint impedance to agonist-antagonist muscle activation commands for key joints (ankle-knee-hip for gait; shoulder-elbow for reaching). Third, a low-level pulse-width modulation module will deliver these commands via a multi-channel FES system. Validation will be performed in simulation using a detailed musculoskeletal model and experimentally with human participants performing constrained reaching and treadmill walking tasks, measuring kinematic accuracy, perturbation rejection, and electromyographic (EMG) cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, biomimetic control framework for multi-joint FES that prioritizes dynamic stability over precise trajectory tracking. It will yield a novel control algorithm that explicitly links task-level stability specifications to muscle activation patterns, advancing the field beyond phenomenological tuning. The results will demonstrate the functional benefits of co-activation-based control in complex, dynamic tasks.
proposals.novelty: While co-activation control has been studied for single joints, its systematic application to coordinate multiple muscles across a kinematic chain for whole-limb FES control is novel. The key innovation is using a model of the task's dynamic constraints to *prescribe* necessary co-activation levels, rather than treating it as an emergent or compensatory phenomenon.

proposals.title: Bilateral Impedance Shaping for Coordinated Standing: A Primitive-Based Control Framework
proposals.motivation: Human bilateral coordination, such as quiet standing, involves complex, co-activated control of two limbs to manage interaction forces and maintain balance. Pre-2026 work by Hogan and colleagues on shaping endpoint impedance for single-limb tasks and using simple primitives for complex dynamics provides a foundation. However, a principled framework for scaling these ideas to bilateral coordination, where the task dynamics are defined by the coupling between limbs and the environment, remains an open challenge with implications for robotics and rehabilitation.
proposals.hypothesis: Bilateral motor coordination for standing can be achieved by the concurrent execution of simple, limb-specific mechanical primitives—specifically, tunable endpoint impedances—whose parameters are co-adapted to satisfy the coupled, constrained dynamics of the bilateral task.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational model where each leg is controlled as a mass-spring-damper system (an impedance) acting at the foot-ground interface, analogous to the 'shaping impedances' framework. The primitives' stiffness, damping, and equilibrium positions will be governed by low-dimensional, coupled oscillators, inspired by the 'simple primitive actions' used in whip control. Human motion capture and force plate data from bilateral standing and sway tasks will be used to identify the 'preferred' velocity or force patterns, informing the oscillator coupling rules. Stability and performance will be analyzed using tools from constrained dynamical systems theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control-theoretic model of bilateral coordination grounded in impedance modulation, directly extending Hogan's unilateral principles. It will yield a testable framework for how the nervous system might solve bilateral coordination problems through mechanical interaction, with potential applications in the design of bilateral assistive devices and prosthetics.
proposals.novelty: While impedance control is well-studied for single limbs, applying it concurrently to two limbs with explicit coordination rules for co-adaptation to a shared task constraint is novel. The approach uniquely combines the 'simple primitives' concept with bilateral coupled-oscillator dynamics to explain whole-body balance.

proposals.title: Minimal Co-activation for Task-Compliant Impedance: An Optimization Framework for Efficient, Smooth Movement
proposals.motivation: Muscle co-activation is a known strategy for stabilizing limb mechanics and shaping endpoint impedance to comply with task dynamics, as shown in Hogan's work. However, sustained co-activation is metabolically costly and contributes to fatigue. A principled method to find the minimal co-activation necessary for smooth, stable task execution would bridge the gap between impedance control theory and energetic efficiency, with applications in rehabilitation and ergonomics.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given task-defined vector field (e.g., a velocity field guiding motion to a target), there exists a unique, minimal level of antagonistic muscle co-activation that ensures both stability against perturbations and the smooth kinematic trajectories observed in human movement, such as those characterized by bell-shaped velocity profiles and low jerk.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework using a planar, two-joint, six-muscle (mono- and bi-articular) neuromusculoskeletal model. The task will be defined as a stable, first-order dynamical system (e.g., a vector field converging to a target from Hogan's 'shaping impedances' work). Using direct collocation optimal control, we will solve for muscle activation patterns that minimize a cost function combining the integral of squared muscle stress (a proxy for metabolic cost) and kinematic jerk, subject to the constraints of producing the task-specified stable vector field and rejecting a set of predefined mechanical perturbations. The solution will reveal the minimal co-activation pattern.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a normative, model-based explanation for the observed levels of co-activation in human motor control, linking task dynamics directly to energetic optimality. It will yield a quantitative design principle for assistive devices or rehabilitation protocols that aim to reduce user fatigue while preserving task performance and stability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has optimized for energy or effort, and other work has shown how impedance is shaped for tasks, this proposal uniquely formulates the simultaneous optimization of both, explicitly seeking the minimal co-activation required to realize a specific, stable task dynamic, which is non-trivial.

proposals.title: Task-Dependent Modulation of Endpoint Stiffness: A Test of Invariance in Dynamic and Unstable Environments
proposals.motivation: A foundational concept in human motor control, stemming from Hogan's impedance control paradigm, is that the central nervous system regulates endpoint stiffness as an invariant strategy to stabilize movement. This assumption of invariance underpins many models of interaction control. However, its validity remains largely untested for tasks requiring explicit modulation of force and stability, such as manipulating a whip or interacting with a cane in a dynamic environment, where task mechanics may demand adaptable stiffness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shape and orientation of the endpoint stiffness ellipse are not invariant but are systematically modulated by task dynamics, particularly in tasks where the mechanical interaction with the environment is explicitly unstable or requires precise force modulation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a planar robotic manipulandum to measure endpoint stiffness via established force perturbation techniques (e.g., stochastic displacement probes). Human subjects will perform two contrasting tasks: 1) a stable, point-to-point reaching task (baseline), and 2) a dynamic task requiring them to control a simulated, underactuated 'whip' or a virtual cane to hit a moving target, as inspired by prior simulation work. We will analyze stiffness ellipses across the workspace and task phases, correlating their orientation and shape with instantaneous motion velocity, applied force vector, and the local stability demands of the task mechanics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence for or against the invariance of stiffness in explicitly dynamic tasks, refining the impedance control theory. It will establish a quantitative link between task-level stability requirements and neuromuscular impedance strategy, with implications for designing more adaptive physical human-robot interaction and rehabilitation protocols.
proposals.novelty: While stiffness has been measured in static postures or simple movements, its systematic modulation in response to the explicit, time-varying instability of a tool-mediated task has not been investigated, challenging a core assumption of motor control models.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Endpoint Stiffness: A Study of Non-Uniform Joint Stiffness Under Task Constraints
proposals.motivation: The endpoint stiffness ellipse is a foundational concept in motor control, used to infer limb stability and impedance control strategies. However, this lumped representation, while convenient, may mask the underlying complexity of the neuromuscular system. If the mapping from joint stiffness to endpoint stiffness is not one-to-one, conclusions about neural control drawn from endpoint ellipses could be misleading, particularly for tasks with complex dynamics or constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a given, uniform endpoint stiffness ellipse can be generated by a wide range of non-uniform joint stiffness configurations, and that humans exploit this redundancy to satisfy task-specific dynamic constraints not captured by the ellipse geometry.
proposals.method: We will employ a planar, two-joint (shoulder, elbow) robotic manipulandum to measure endpoint force-displacement relationships during constrained tasks, such as interacting with a virtual cane or whip-like object in simulation. Using established inverse kinematics and static force models, we will compute the feasible set of joint stiffness matrices consistent with each measured endpoint stiffness. We will analyze human motion, velocity, and force data to identify if the chosen joint stiffnesses correlate with task dynamics (e.g., aligning high joint stiffness with directions of unstable interaction forces) rather than simply shaping a preferred endpoint ellipse.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical re-evaluation of the endpoint stiffness as a complete descriptor of limb impedance, demonstrating its inherent ambiguity. It will offer a new framework for interpreting impedance control data, linking specific joint stiffness patterns to the mechanical requirements of dynamic tasks, thereby bridging the gap between abstract stability measures and behavioral execution.
proposals.novelty: While endpoint stiffness measurement is standard, systematically mapping its degeneracy to joint-space solutions and testing whether humans select solutions based on task dynamics (beyond ellipse geometry) is a novel and non-obvious direction. It challenges the sufficiency of the endpoint representation central to much pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Invariance of Endpoint Stiffness Ellipse Parameters Under Varied External Loads During Postural Tasks
proposals.motivation: The endpoint stiffness ellipse is a fundamental characterization of limb impedance, central to theories of stability and interaction control in human motor control. A key, unresolved question is whether this stiffness is an intrinsic, invariant property of the neuromuscular system for a given posture, or if it is systematically modulated by external task dynamics, such as supporting added loads. Resolving this is critical for refining models of impedance control and for designing assistive devices and rehabilitation protocols that interact with human limbs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shape, size, and orientation of the endpoint stiffness ellipse will remain invariant when subjects maintain an identical posture while supporting added external loads, provided the central nervous system's 'preferred' kinematic setpoint is unchanged.
proposals.method: Subjects will maintain a fixed arm posture while holding a robotic manipulandum. Using established methods from Hogan's lab, endpoint stiffness will be estimated by applying small, stochastic displacement perturbations (a 'white noise' position disturbance) and measuring the resulting restoring forces. This will be repeated under multiple conditions: with no load, and with inertially matched masses added to the manipulandum. The stiffness matrix will be identified from the force-displacement data, and its eigenvalues/eigenvectors (defining the ellipse) will be compared across conditions. Crucially, we will monitor the limb's 'preferred' or spontaneous position drift (a Brownian process) to ensure the kinematic referent is consistent.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct empirical evidence for or against the invariance of stiffness under altered inertial loads, a foundational assumption in some motor control models. It will clarify the separation between intrinsic limb mechanics and voluntary impedance modulation, contributing to more accurate models of human motor control for physical interaction. The findings will also inform the design of co-robotic systems and exoskeletons that must account for human impedance properties.
proposals.novelty: While stiffness has been measured under various conditions, a direct test of its invariance under pure inertial loading—while controlling for the kinematic referent via measurement of spontaneous drift—has not been explicitly conducted. This bridges Hogan's recent work on behavioral consequences of velocity commands (Brownian processes) with his foundational work on impedance control.

proposals.title: Mapping Endpoint Stiffness Ellipses to Muscle Synergies: An EMG-Based Validation of Impedance Control Strategies
proposals.motivation: The mechanical impedance of the human arm, characterized by stiffness ellipses, is fundamental to stable interaction with objects and environments. While Hogan's paradigm of impedance control is well-established, the direct, quantitative mapping from measured muscle activation patterns to the features of these stiffness ellipses remains incomplete. This gap limits our ability to decode the neuromuscular strategies underlying the 'shaping of impedances to comply with constrained task dynamics'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific features of the endpoint stiffness ellipse (orientation, shape, and size) will be linearly correlated with the activation levels of identified muscle synergies, and that these correlations will shift predictably between tasks requiring different stability constraints, such as point-to-point reaching versus contact with a rigid surface.
proposals.method: Participants will perform planar reaching and force-application tasks using a robotic manipulandum (e.g., Kinarm) that can apply perturbations to estimate endpoint stiffness ellipses. Simultaneous high-density EMG will be recorded from major shoulder and elbow muscles. Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) will extract muscle synergies from EMG patterns. We will employ multivariate linear regression and canonical correlation analysis to model the relationship between synergy activation coefficients (the independent variables) and the ellipse features (major/minor axes, orientation). The analysis will specifically test for changes in these correlations under different task dynamics, linking to the concept of 'preferred' motion paths emerging from impedance shaping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, empirical link between the neuromechanical (EMG) and task-mechanical (stiffness) levels of description in motor control. It will validate and quantify impedance control theory at the muscle synergy level, offering a more granular understanding of how the CNS implements stability. The results will inform the design of biomimetic controllers for assistive robots and prostheses that can modulate impedance naturally.
proposals.novelty: While stiffness estimation and synergy extraction are established separately, their direct correlation in the context of constrained task dynamics is novel. This approach moves beyond describing stiffness as a black-box mechanical output to revealing its underlying muscle-level construction rules, integrating Hogan's impedance control framework with modern synergy analysis.

proposals.title: Robust Identification of Endpoint Stiffness in Human Arm Control Using Recursive Least Squares and Instrumental Variable Methods
proposals.motivation: Accurate estimation of limb endpoint stiffness is crucial for understanding how humans achieve stable, compliant interaction with dynamic environments, a central theme in Hogan's work on impedance control. Traditional methods, like static perturbations or regression from force-displacement data, are sensitive to measurement noise and unmodeled dynamics, limiting their reliability for studying tasks like cane use or whip manipulation. Applying established system identification techniques from robotics can provide a more rigorous, noise-resistant framework for quantifying the mechanical impedance humans shape during task execution.
proposals.hypothesis: Robotic parameter identification algorithms, specifically Recursive Least Squares (RLS) with forgetting factors and Instrumental Variable (IV) methods, can robustly estimate the full, time-varying endpoint stiffness matrix of the human arm from motion and force data, even in the presence of sensor noise and voluntary movement. This will reveal that the identified stiffness ellipsoids align more consistently with the constrained task dynamics than estimates from simpler linear regression.
proposals.method: We will collect kinematic (endpoint position, velocity) and kinetic (interaction force) data from participants performing planar reaching and posture-holding tasks with and without external perturbations using a robotic manipulandum. The arm's dynamics will be modeled as a mass-spring-damper system. We will implement and compare two identification routines: 1) an RLS algorithm to adaptively estimate stiffness and damping parameters from the equation of motion, and 2) an IV method to mitigate bias caused by noise in the velocity and acceleration signals. Algorithms will be validated in simulation with known parameters before application to human data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, methodologically robust tool for the experimental identification of biological impedance, directly extending Hogan's foundational work on shaping impedances to comply with tasks. It will yield higher-fidelity stiffness estimates to better correlate with behavioral phenomena like preferred movement paths or stochastic processes in motor output. The resulting techniques will be applicable to studying a wider range of dynamic physical interactions, from tool use to rehabilitation.
proposals.novelty: While system identification is standard in robotics, its application to directly estimate the full, time-varying endpoint stiffness matrix in human motor control is novel. The key innovation is treating the neuromuscular system as a parameter-varying mechanical system to be identified online, moving beyond static or averaged stiffness approximations common in prior psychophysical studies.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop System Identification of Arm Endpoint Impedance During Unconstrained, Velocity-Based Reaching Tasks
proposals.motivation: Human motor control is characterized by the modulation of limb impedance to interact stably and efficiently with the environment. While prior work by Hogan and others has characterized endpoint stiffness and damping in static or constrained postures, the dynamic impedance transfer function during unconstrained, voluntary motion—particularly under velocity-based control strategies—remains poorly quantified. A precise, time-varying model of arm impedance is crucial for understanding how the nervous system manages task dynamics and for designing more responsive physical human-robot interfaces and rehabilitation protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: The arm's endpoint impedance during goal-directed reaching can be accurately modeled as a linear time-varying (LTV) system, and its parameters will reveal a systematic relationship to the instantaneous hand velocity and proximity to the task-defined preferred movement path.
proposals.method: We will employ closed-loop system identification techniques from control theory. Participants will perform planar reaching movements in a robotic manipulandum (e.g., Kinarm) while receiving small, stochastic multi-axis force perturbations. The commanded hand velocity and resulting kinematic error will be recorded. Using pre-2026 algorithms such as Ensemble Linear Time-Varying (LTV) identification or Recursive Least Squares with forgetting factors, we will estimate the time-varying inertia, damping, and stiffness matrices constituting the endpoint impedance transfer function. The analysis will explicitly correlate impedance parameters with instantaneous velocity and deviation from the subject's preferred movement center.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, time-domain identification of the full endpoint impedance transfer function during voluntary motion, moving beyond static or averaged characterizations. It will quantitatively link Hogan's concepts of "shaping impedances" and "preferred movement paths" by showing how impedance is dynamically tuned in relation to an ongoing velocity command and task error.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying formal, closed-loop LTV system identification to the unconstrained arm during active movement, explicitly integrating the analysis with the velocity-command framework and the concept of a preferred behavioral path, which have been studied separately in prior motor control work.

proposals.title: Inferring Multi-Joint Impedance and Torque Profiles During Unconstrained, Dynamic Tool Use
proposals.motivation: Understanding how the nervous system controls interaction forces during dynamic, unconstrained tasks is a core challenge in motor neuroscience. Prior work by Hogan and colleagues on impedance control and task dynamics has often focused on constrained, planar interactions or simplified models. Extending this to naturalistic, three-dimensional tasks like wielding a cane or whip is essential to bridge the gap between fundamental control principles and real-world human performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during unconstrained, dynamic tool manipulation, the central nervous system shapes task-specific, multi-joint impedance profiles that correlate with the computed joint torque patterns required to achieve the preferred motion trajectory.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution, multi-camera motion capture (e.g., Vicon) with a handheld instrumented cane equipped with a 6-axis force/torque sensor. Full-arm kinematics (shoulder, elbow, wrist) will be recorded. Joint angles and velocities will be computed via inverse kinematics, and net joint torques will be estimated using a recursive Newton-Euler inverse dynamics algorithm applied to a subject-specific biomechanical model. The temporal structure of joint torque and preferred motion velocity profiles will be analyzed using tools from Brownian process analysis to identify control strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, empirical quantification of upper-limb joint torque and inferred impedance during truly unconstrained, dynamic tool manipulation. It will test the generality of the equilibrium-point and impedance control frameworks in a more ecological context and offer a methodological pipeline for analyzing complex skill acquisition, such as learning to manipulate a whip.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of full 3D inverse dynamics from motion capture with force measurement for unconstrained tool-use tasks, moving beyond constrained planar robots to test established motor control theories in a naturalistic setting that matches the complexity of the behaviors they aim to explain.

proposals.title: Scaling of Endpoint Stiffness with Tool Inertia and Task Dynamics in Human Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on human motor control, including Hogan's work on impedance, has established that the nervous system modulates limb stiffness to stabilize interactions with the environment. However, the principles governing how hand stiffness scales when wielding tools of varying dynamic complexity (e.g., mass, inertia) remain unclear. Understanding this scaling is crucial for developing robust robotic and prosthetic controllers that can handle a range of objects and tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hand endpoint stiffness will increase non-linearly with the effective inertia of a held tool, and that this scaling will be task-dependent, shaped to comply with the constrained task dynamics as predicted by the equilibrium-point control framework.
proposals.method: We will adapt the established 'position-servo' or 'force-perturbation' method to measure hand stiffness ellipses. Participants will hold and stabilize tools of known but varying mass and moment of inertia against a planar robotic manipulandum. The manipulandum will apply small, stochastic force perturbations (modeled as a Brownian process) during different stabilization tasks (e.g., maintaining a point, tracing a path). Stiffness will be estimated from the regression of restoring force against displacement, with analysis focusing on how ellipse geometry and magnitude scale with tool inertia and task-defined velocity constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model of stiffness scaling with object dynamics, directly linking Hogan's impedance control theory to tool use. It will offer empirical data to refine models of internal model formation and validate control strategies for complex, underactuated systems (like whips) studied in simulation. The findings will inform the design of assistive devices that automatically adapt impedance based on grasped object properties.
proposals.novelty: While stiffness modulation is known, the systematic quantification of its scaling with the complex inertial properties of a tool, and its dissociation from simple co-contraction, is novel. This moves beyond characterizing stiffness in free space or with fixed objects to the dynamic, tool-mediated interactions central to real-world manipulation.

proposals.title: Motion-Primary Control: A Constrained Optimization Framework for Task Performance with Impedance Bounds
proposals.motivation: Traditional impedance control treats desired mechanical impedance as a primary control objective, shaping it to stabilize interaction, with motion emerging as a secondary consequence. However, many real-world tasks, such as using a cane for navigation or manipulating a whip, have a clear, primary kinematic or dynamic motion goal, while the impedance the human or robot can achieve is physiologically or mechanically constrained. A framework that inverts this relationship is needed to understand how biological systems and robots can best perform tasks under inherent impedance limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating desired task motion as the primary optimization objective, subject to the constraint of realizable endpoint impedance, will yield control strategies that better explain human performance in constrained dynamic tasks and generate more effective robotic controllers than traditional impedance-first approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a constrained optimization framework where the cost function minimizes a task-specific motion error (e.g., deviation from a desired whip-tip trajectory or cane-tip force profile), subject to inequality constraints defining the feasible space of endpoint impedance (stiffness, damping, inertia). The solution will be implemented in simulation using model predictive control (MPC) with a human arm or robot dynamic model. We will validate the approach by comparing simulated controllers to human experimental data from tasks like rhythmic cane tapping or whip manipulation, analyzing the predicted versus measured center of motion and velocity profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational principle for motor control that formally prioritizes task dynamics over impedance shaping. It will yield testable predictions about how humans manage impedance constraints and offer a novel controller design paradigm for robots performing complex, underactuated dynamic tasks where perfect impedance modulation is impossible.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the canonical impedance control paradigm by treating motion as the objective and impedance as a constraint, rather than vice-versa. It synthesizes Hogan's foundational work on impedance with modern constrained optimization methods to address tasks where motion is paramount and impedance is limited.

proposals.title: Operational Space Dynamics for Task-Level Impedance Shaping: A Unified Framework for Human-Inspired Control
proposals.motivation: Impedance control is fundamental for compliant human-robot interaction and understanding human motor adaptation, but deriving task-specific impedance laws for complex, redundant manipulators remains analytically cumbersome. The operational space formulation, well-established in robotics for force control, provides a rigorous mathematical framework for describing task-space dynamics, yet its potential to systematically simplify and unify impedance control law derivation for constrained tasks is underexplored. This gap limits the design of controllers that can replicate the elegant task-specific impedance modulation observed in human movement, such as when using tools or interacting with unstable environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the operational space dynamics formulation to the problem of impedance shaping will yield a unified and algebraically simplified control law that directly maps task dynamics constraints to required joint-space impedances, thereby explaining human strategies for tool use and environmental interaction.
proposals.method: We will derive the operational space dynamics for a simulated planar multi-joint arm (or leg) model interacting with a task-defined virtual object, such as a cane or a whip. The task-space impedance objective (e.g., stabilizing a point on a whip tip or a cane-ground contact) will be specified as a desired inertia, damping, and stiffness in operational coordinates. Using the dynamically consistent operational space inertia matrix and its associated null-space projection, we will algebraically solve for the joint-space torque command that realizes this task-space impedance while accommodating redundancy. This derivation will be implemented in simulation and validated by comparing its emergent behavior to human motion data from constrained tasks, analyzing metrics like endpoint force profiles and velocity distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, generalizable control-theoretic framework for deriving task-specific impedances, directly linking the biomechanics of tool use to established robotics theory. It will offer new predictions for human motor control, explaining how the nervous system might implicitly solve for impedance parameters to comply with complex task dynamics, thereby bridging Hogan's impedance control paradigm with operational space methods.
proposals.novelty: While operational space control is known for force/position control, its explicit application to *systematically derive and simplify* task-space impedance control laws for biomechanical models and human-inspired tasks represents a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond ad-hoc impedance selection to a formal derivation grounded in the separation of task and postural dynamics.

ideas.description: This system combines a patient-specific task-space capability model with a simplified whole-body dynamic model to predict stability and interaction forces during gait. Using a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, it generates personalized gait training tasks (for treadmill or overground robots) that explicitly optimize for dynamic stability, safety, and therapeutic challenge within the patient's predicted physical limits. This approach ensures rehabilitation strategies are not only capability-matched but also inherently balance-aware, preventing unsafe perturbations and promoting biomechanically sound movement recovery.

ideas.title: Model-Calibrated Neuromechanical Fusion for Robust Efforce Estimation
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond using biosignals as a direct effort proxy by fusing EMG or other neural intent signals with mechanical sensors (force/torque transducers, inertial measurement units) in a closed-loop estimation framework. A real-time adaptive model learns the individual user's dynamic mapping from muscle activation to mechanical output, continuously calibrating for states like fatigue or pain that cause dissociation. The resulting calibrated 'effort' signal is a robust, personalized estimate of intended mechanical contribution, which is then used for the greedy update in assistive or rehabilitation control. This approach ensures the effort estimation remains valid even as the user's physiological state changes during a session.

ideas.title: Intermittent Contact Telerehabilitation: Home-Based Therapy via Simplified Non-Continuous Manipulation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the design of home-based telerehabilitation systems that abandon traditional continuous mechanical coupling between the assistive device and the patient's limb. Instead, it leverages simplified, robust controllers (like the GAAN) to orchestrate therapeutic manipulation through sequences of intermittent physical contacts (e.g., from a repositionable padded actuator) or non-mechanical coupling such as focused jets of air or brief tactile stimuli. This approach inherently enhances safety in unpredictable home environments by eliminating persistent rigid links, reducing complexity and cost. The core challenge becomes developing control strategies that achieve therapeutic motion and resistance training through these discrete or field-based interactions, validated for efficacy and user safety without direct clinician supervision.

ideas.title: Adaptive Gait Rehabilitation via Ecological Resource Partitioning Strategy
ideas.description: This idea scales up lower-limb gait rehabilitation by drawing an analogy from community ecology's solution to resource competition. In ecology, species coexist by partitioning resources (niches) to minimize conflict and maximize system efficiency. The analogous solution uses models like niche theory to find these optimal partitions. Adapted to overground or treadmill gait training, we model the patient's diverse, impaired neuromuscular capabilities as agents competing to contribute to the gait task. The task-space capability model defines the 'movement resource.' A greedy strategy, inspired by iterative niche-finding algorithms, sequentially identifies and trains the most underutilized or critical motor capability (e.g., training knee control before demanding more from an already-weak hip), thereby 'partitioning' the gait demand across the sensorimotor system. This promotes adaptive, stable gait recovery by systematically building a coordinated, efficient movement 'ecosystem' rather than over-stressing a single component.

ideas.title: Dual-State Estimation for Degenerative Disease: Decoupling Effort from Capability via Biosignal Fusion
ideas.description: This research proposes a unified model that fuses real-time physiological signals (e.g., EMG, EEG, or force) with longitudinal motor performance data to solve a dual estimation problem. The core innovation is the development of an adaptive filter or probabilistic model that, during a task, jointly infers the patient's current 'effort state' and their deteriorating 'capability state'. By decoupling these two latent variables, the model can provide a purified, effort-corrected trajectory of degenerative disease progression. Furthermore, it enables the personalization of rehabilitation by distinguishing between poor performance due to lack of effort and performance limited by genuine physiological decline.

ideas.title: Adapt Metabolic Control Theory for Online Impedance Tuning in Manipulation
ideas.description: This idea transfers the core problem-solving approach from Metabolic Control Analysis in Systems Biology to the domain of robotic manipulation. In metabolic engineering, MCA is used to understand and adaptively control complex, interconnected biochemical networks by identifying and modulating high-leverage parameters (enzyme activities) to optimize output fluxes. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing a robot's manipulation task as a network where impedance parameters govern the 'flux' of task performance. The research would develop a novel online impedance tuning framework that, inspired by MCA, continuously estimates the sensitivity of task performance to each impedance parameter and prioritizes adaptive adjustments to those with the highest control, enabling robust and efficient adaptation to unknown environments and varying contact dynamics.

ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the data scarcity flaw by combining a generative biomechanical simulation with meta-learning. A high-fidelity physics simulator creates a diverse synthetic population of hand impairments, providing the necessary training data for the GAAN paradigm in a controlled, ethical manner. The meta-learned policy allows the exoskeleton's GAAN controller to quickly personalize its assistance strategy to a new patient after a brief calibration, adapting to their unique anatomy and residual motor function. This results in a robust system that maintains the benefits of multi-agent training while being practical for clinical deployment with individual patients.

ideas.title: Dual: Adversarial Resistance for Guaranteed Muscular Overload
ideas.description: This system applies an adversarial resistance principle to robotic strength training, designed to guarantee a target level of muscular overload rather than merely assisting with task completion. It uses a real-time biomechanical and fatigue-state model to dynamically apply precisely calibrated opposing forces throughout an exercise's range of motion, ensuring the working muscle maintains a pre-defined high level of effort (e.g., 85% of MVC). This approach directly enforces the progressive overload principle by modulating external resistance to compensate for strength curves and fatigue, transforming the robot from a helper into an intelligent adversary that optimizes the mechanical stimulus for strength and hypertrophy adaptations.

ideas.title: Adaptive Impedance Control Inspired by Immune System Self/Non-Self Discrimination
ideas.description: This research adapts the immune system's self/non-self discrimination principle to robotic manipulation. In immunology, the body initially assumes an isolated, self-state but possesses a learned, adaptive response mechanism triggered only upon confirmed contact with foreign entities. Analogously, we propose a control architecture where the manipulator operates under a high-performance, pre-computed model that assumes isolation from the environment. A parallel 'immune-inspired' surveillance module continuously monitors sensor data (joint torques, accelerations). When this module detects a persistent signature inconsistent with the isolated dynamics model—indicating intentional or accidental contact—it activates a separate, learned impedance adaptation policy. This policy, potentially trained via reinforcement learning in simulation across varied contact scenarios, modulates the controller's stiffness and damping in real-time to achieve stable interaction, effectively 'learning' an appropriate response to different contact types while defaulting to the high-performance isolated controller when no contact is detected.

ideas.title: A Home-Based, Self-Calibrating Telerehabilitation System Using Adaptive Filters for Real-Time Capability Estimation and Controller Tuning
ideas.description: This research develops a telerehabilitation system for safe, unsupervised home use that integrates adaptive filtering (e.g., Kalman Filters) directly into the control loop. The filters dynamically estimate both the patient's latent capability state and the parameters of a simplified, robust GAAN controller from noisy, low-cost sensor data. This dual estimation allows the system to self-calibrate to each patient and autonomously adjust assistance levels in real-time without clinician intervention, ensuring safety and efficacy. The result is a truly adaptive home system that provides personalized therapy by continuously refining its internal patient model and control policy based on observed performance.

ideas.title: Efficient Online Policy Adaptation via Lightweight RL with Interpretable Patient Models
ideas.description: This research direction combines the efficiency of simple, interpretable function approximators (like linear models or decision trees) for modeling patient motor capability with a reinforcement learning framework for policy optimization. The lightweight patient model is updated online with minimal computational cost, providing a rapidly evolving state representation for the RL agent. This allows the RL policy to adapt assistance in real-time based on the interpretable features of the patient model, enabling personalized therapy that balances challenge and support. The result is a system that achieves the long-term optimization benefits of RL without the prohibitive computational burden of complex models, making it feasible for embedded or clinical deployment.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptive Control via Probabilistic Heterogeneous Capability Maps
ideas.description: This approach designs rehabilitation or assistive controllers that explicitly model extreme inter- and intra-patient heterogeneity as a time-varying stochastic field. Instead of a static, finely-tuned map, the system maintains a probabilistic belief over a patient's task-space capabilities, which it updates in real-time from sensor data. The control policy is then optimized for robust performance across this belief distribution, ensuring graceful degradation and safe adaptation to capability changes due to recovery, fatigue, or other factors. This creates a system that leverages heterogeneity while being fundamentally resilient to its inherent uncertainty and temporal evolution.

ideas.title: Adaptive Kalman Filter Fusion of Multi-Modal Biosignals for Effort Estimation
ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the problem of direct patient effort estimation by drawing an analogy from aerospace engineering's sensor fusion techniques. In inertial navigation, a Kalman filter is used to optimally combine data from multiple imperfect sensors to estimate a system's true state. Adapted to this biomedical context, we treat various biosignals (EMG, ECG, etc.) as sensors measuring the latent variable 'effort'. The research would develop an adaptive Kalman filter framework that fuses these multi-modal physiological signals in real-time. The filter would incorporate a physiological model to predict relationships between signals and effort, dynamically adjusting the weighting of each signal based on its estimated noise and relevance. This provides a robust, continuous, and model-informed estimate of patient effort, which can then be used to drive the greedy update in adaptive therapeutic or human-computer interaction systems, improving responsiveness and personalization.

ideas.title: Apply Filter Design Synthesis to Mechanical Impedance Matching
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from digital signal processing and RF filter design to the problem of mechanical impedance design. In those fields, a desired frequency response is synthesized into a physical electrical circuit using formal network synthesis techniques. The adapted research idea is to treat a target mechanical impedance profile—specifying how a system should respond to force inputs across different frequencies—as an analogous 'transfer function' to be synthesized. The core methodological transfer involves developing a synthesis algorithm that, given a target impedance function, outputs a realizable configuration and parameter set for a network of passive mechanical elements (springs, dampers, inerters). This would provide a systematic, mathematically rigorous framework for designing mechanical impedances with complex, tailored frequency-domain behaviors, moving beyond trial-and-error or simplified models.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Neural Bond Graphs for Learning Nonlinear Contact Dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid modeling framework that combines the structural and energetic clarity of bond graphs with the function approximation power of neural networks to create identifiable models of nonlinear manipulator-environment interactions. The bond graph defines the system's energy ports and state equations, providing a physics-based scaffold that guarantees passivity and correct interconnection. Neural networks are then embedded as the constitutive laws for nonlinear resistive, capacitive, and transformer elements, with their training rigorously constrained by the port-Hamiltonian dynamics derived from the graph. This approach results in models that are both interpretable in their energy exchange topology and highly accurate in capturing complex, data-driven contact phenomena like friction and deformation.

ideas.title: Guardian-Augmented Adaptive Networks for Safe Unsupervised Home Telerehabilitation
ideas.description: This research develops a Guardian module that integrates with existing GAAN-based telerehabilitation systems to enable safe, long-term home use. The Guardian employs a lightweight, real-time multi-modal sensor fusion pipeline and a probabilistic patient-environment model to continuously monitor for safety-critical states (e.g., incorrect posture, fatigue, environmental hazards) and sub-therapeutic exercise execution. Upon detecting an anomaly, it can execute a graduated response—from subtle haptic guidance and exercise parameter adjustment to session pausing and automated alerting of a remote monitoring center. This approach maintains the GAAN's sophisticated personalization while adding a necessary layer of robustness for the unpredictable home environment, creating a system that is both adaptive and inherently safe.

ideas.title: Challenge the Greedy Strategy with Ecological Resource Management Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a greedy strategy maximizing force leads to optimal motor learning by drawing an analogy from ecological resource management. In that field, maximizing a single output (e.g., crop yield) greedily leads to system degradation, solved by implementing multi-dimensional constraints for sustainability. Adapted here, the research will test if a greedy strategy maximizing force encourages undesirable compensatory movements instead of desired patterns. The experiment will involve a motor task where subjects can use a compensatory, 'force-greedy' strategy, but this will be penalized by a deterioration in a concurrently measured stability or efficiency metric. The solution is to design the task's reward structure to balance force production with this secondary metric, mimicking ecological management principles, to see if this forces the motor system to abandon the greedy local optimum and discover a globally better movement pattern.

ideas.title: Hybrid Topology Optimization and Active Feedback for Realizable Mechanical Impedance Synthesis
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fundamental realizability gap in direct circuit synthesis by separating the problem into topology generation and active compensation. First, a computational search generates a physically realizable passive mechanical network (springs, dampers, inertias, and levers) that best approximates the desired impedance using a constrained optimization that respects non-reciprocity and passivity. Second, an integrated sensor-actuator layer applies model-based feedback control to the physical network, imposing the precise target impedance by actively compensating for nonlinearities, losses, and model mismatch. This creates a composite system where the passive network provides a stable, efficient baseline behavior, and the active layer guarantees precise impedance matching.

ideas.title: Personalized Control via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea inverts the task-space homogeneity assumption by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, species distribution models (like MaxEnt) learn the unique environmental niche of a species from sparse, presence-only data, without assuming habitat uniformity. The analogous solution is to model a patient's motor capability not as a uniform space but as a unique 'capability niche' defined by a probability distribution over kinematic and dynamic variables. The adapted research idea is to design a controller that learns this niche model from a patient's observed movement attempts—including their limits and failures—and uses it to generate personalized assistance. The controller's goal is to facilitate task completion by guiding movements strictly within the high-probability region of the patient's own niche, thereby leveraging extreme heterogeneity and avoiding assumptions of homogeneous capability.

ideas.title: Context-aware challenge adjustment integrating performance, self-report, and passive data
ideas.description: This research investigates a challenge adjustment system that moves beyond performance-only metrics to prevent frustration in patients with high variability. The system dynamically integrates real-time performance data with patient-reported contextual factors (mood, fatigue) and passive behavioral data from wearables to interpret performance fluctuations. This allows the algorithm to distinguish between a genuine change in ability (warranting challenge adjustment) and a temporary state influenced by external factors (warranting supportive adaptation). The study will measure patient frustration, perceived fairness, and engagement compared to a performance-only adjustment system.

ideas.title: Adaptive Filtering for Patient State Estimation, Inspired by Robotic SLAM
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from robotic Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) to the clinical problem of patient capability estimation. In SLAM, particle filters (like FastSLAM) are used to simultaneously estimate a robot's hidden state (localization) and the structure of its unknown environment (mapping) from sequential, noisy sensor data. Analogously, we propose a patient capability SLAM framework. Here, the hidden 'state' is the patient's true, latent functional capability (e.g., motor function, cognitive load capacity), and the 'map' is a personalized model of task difficulty or environmental demand. A particle filter would dynamically fuse noisy, sparse measurements—such as standardized clinical scores, therapist observations, and continuous data from wearables—to maintain a multi-hypothesis probability distribution over the joint patient-state-and-task-map. This allows for robust, real-time estimation of capability trends and personalized adjustment of therapeutic challenges, even amidst significant measurement uncertainty and individual variability.

ideas.title: Adaptive Challenge Algorithms with Game-Based Engagement Feedback to Shape Motor Learning
ideas.description: This research synthesizes serious game engagement analytics with motor learning challenge design. It proposes an adaptive algorithm that monitors a user's engagement and motivation metrics (e.g., from in-game telemetry and subjective feedback) during a force-production task. When the system detects engagement dropping or a user employing a greedy, compensatory strategy to maximize force output, it dynamically modifies the challenge parameters (e.g., by introducing secondary cognitive tasks, altering visual feedback, or shifting reward criteria) to re-engage the user and steer them toward practicing the desired, specific motor pattern. This creates a synergistic loop where maintaining high engagement becomes the mechanism for ensuring high-quality motor practice, moving beyond simple performance maximization to optimized learning.

ideas.title: Stability-Guaranteed Adaptive Nonlinear Impedance Control with Coupling Compensation
ideas.description: This research develops a control framework for nonlinear impedance that explicitly manages the destabilizing interactions between superimposed components. The system continuously monitors for emergent, unmodeled dynamics arising from nonlinear coupling using real-time passivity and stability metrics. An adaptive safety layer intervenes to modulate or decouple components when hazardous interactions are detected, ensuring robust performance. Furthermore, the framework incorporates a learning module to build a predictive model of these coupling effects, enabling proactive compensation and the safe exploration of more complex nonlinear impedance superpositions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control Theory for Patient Challenge Systems
ideas.description: This research seed investigates if adjusting therapeutic or training challenge based solely on immediate performance data frustrates patients with high performance variability. Drawing an analogy from Control Systems Engineering, where adaptive controllers must avoid instability from noisy feedback signals, the analogous solution involves filtering or smoothing the input to respond to trends, not fluctuations. The adapted idea is to design a challenge adjustment algorithm that uses a smoothed trend (e.g., a moving average) of patient performance or a statistical significance threshold, thereby only modifying the challenge level when a sustained change in patient capability is inferred, which may improve engagement and reduce frustration for variable performers.

ideas.title: Robustness-Centric Validation of Impedance Control Frameworks via Multi-Fidelity Failure Mode Analysis
ideas.description: This research systematically identifies and addresses the practical failure modes of theoretical impedance control frameworks to ensure robust real-world application. It develops a comprehensive taxonomy of potential failures—spanning hardware limitations, model inaccuracies, and unpredictable environmental contacts—and constructs a corresponding validation pipeline. The approach employs high-fidelity simulation and a purpose-built, reconfigurable hardware platform to test the framework's limits and adaptability across a representative set of manipulator designs and task scenarios. The outcome is a rigorously stress-tested theoretical extension accompanied by clear guidelines for its reliable implementation in Part II and III applications.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Rebalancing RL for Personalized Rehabilitation Scheduling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a reinforcement learning solution from computational finance—specifically, dynamic portfolio rebalancing—to the domain of patient care. In finance, RL agents learn optimal policies to sequentially rebalance a portfolio of assets, maximizing long-term risk-adjusted returns under uncertainty and transaction costs. Adapted to rehabilitation, the RL agent would learn an optimal assistance policy that dynamically 'rebalances' a patient's therapeutic 'portfolio' (e.g., exercises, activities, rest). The agent interacts with a patient model (simulated or real) to sequentially adjust therapy parameters, aiming to maximize a long-term, composite metric of patient progress while minimizing costs like fatigue or disengagement, thereby personalizing the rehabilitation trajectory in a data-driven way.

ideas.title: Successive Linearization for Mildly Nonlinear Robotic Manipulation
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'frozen nonlinearity' or small-perturbation linearization method from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the domain of robotic manipulation with mild nonlinearities. In CFD, complex turbulent flows are often analyzed by linearizing the governing equations around a known mean flow, enabling efficient computation of perturbations. Similarly, we propose a control architecture for manipulation tasks where the full system dynamics (robot + object + environment) are nonlinear but deviations from a planned nominal trajectory are expected to be small. The method involves: 1) planning a dynamically feasible nominal trajectory using the full nonlinear model, 2) constructing a linear time-varying (LTV) approximation of the dynamics around this trajectory, 3) employing a fast linear optimal controller (like an LQR tracker) to stabilize deviations, and 4) implementing a triggering mechanism to recompute the nominal path and re-linearize only when tracking error or model mismatch exceeds a predefined threshold. This approach challenges the assumption that effective control for mildly nonlinear systems requires either full nonlinear optimization at every step or overly conservative linear controllers, instead offering a hybrid solution that balances accuracy and computational speed.

ideas.title: Adapting Industrial Fault-Tolerant Control for Home-Based Telerehabilitation
ideas.description: This idea scales the GAAN controller for home-based telerehabilitation by drawing an analogy from Industrial Process Control, specifically Fault-Tolerant Control (FTC) systems. In industrial settings, FTC ensures safe operation by non-experts through layered protocols and 'graceful degradation'—automatically switching to safe, predefined modes during anomalies. Adapted to the research context, the GAAN controller would be simplified with a core 'safety supervisor' module. This module continuously monitors patient movement, sensor data integrity, and system states. If it detects potential risks (e.g., erratic motion suggesting fatigue, loss of camera feed, or communication latency), it would automatically override the complex adaptive controller and transition the rehabilitation device into a verified, low-risk 'safe mode.' This fail-safe adaptation allows the system to be safely used unsupervised at home, as it can manage unexpected events without requiring immediate expert intervention, thereby scaling the technology from clinical to domestic settings.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Pest Management's Controlled Disruption to Motor Learning
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from Integrated Pest Management in agricultural science, where 'controlled disruption' is used to break stable, undesirable ecological cycles. The analogous solution involves strategically perturbing a system (e.g., with sterile insects or crop rotation) to guide it toward a more desirable and self-regulating state, rather than attempting total eradication. Adapted to the AAN challenge logic, this translates to incorporating motor learning principles like error augmentation and variable practice not merely as corrective tools, but as forms of controlled disruption. The system would be designed to strategically destabilize the learner's current, suboptimal motor schema, thereby pushing the neural motor control system to explore and settle into a more robust, efficient, and adaptable movement pattern. This approach reframes the challenge from error minimization to guided system perturbation for long-term adaptive gain.

ideas.title: Robust personalization via dual-timescale Bayesian filtering of recovery dynamics
ideas.description: This system personalizes intervention strategies by maintaining two coupled Bayesian filters: a fast filter tracks observed patient states with associated measurement noise, while a slow filter estimates the patient's underlying, stable recovery parameters. The adaptation rate for the greedy strategy is then modulated by the confidence (inverse variance) of the slow filter's estimate, ensuring updates are only made when a change in recovery profile is statistically credible. This approach prevents overreaction to transient fluctuations and grounds personalization in robust, long-term evidence.

ideas.title: Causal Isolation of GAAN Efficacy via Controlled Reversal and Digital Phenotyping in Long-Term Trials
ideas.description: This trial redesign integrates an adaptive, multi-arm protocol to causally isolate the GAAN controller's effect over extended periods. It includes randomized, scheduled 'controller-off' intervals to establish a reversal-of-effect baseline, differentiating the controller's contribution from natural history and concurrent care. Continuous, passive digital phenotyping (actigraphy, voice, daily task performance) provides a high-resolution, objective dataset to model confounding variables and quantify functional recovery dynamics. The combined approach yields a robust, causal efficacy signal, validating not just correlation but the specific therapeutic impact of the closed-loop controller.

ideas.title: Intermittent Manipulation via Field-Mediated Synchronization
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that robotic manipulation requires continuous mechanical coupling or contact. Drawing an analogy from Quantum Optics and Cavity QED, where atoms are manipulated via intermittent interactions with a shared cavity field, we propose a manipulation strategy using a shared, tunable physical field (acoustic, magnetic, or airflow) as a mediating medium. The analogous solution involves using the field as a temporary information bus for synchronized, discrete control. Adapted to this context, a manipulator would apply brief, pulsed interactions with the field—rather than maintaining continuous contact—to impart specific momenta or forces to an object, enabling tasks like reorientation, sorting, or assembly through sequences of timed, non-mechanical impulses. This approach studies the control paradigms, field design, and timing strategies required for reliable manipulation via intermittent, field-mediated coupling.

ideas.title: Adapt Ecological Resilience Monitoring for GAAN Long-Term Trial Validation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the long-term ecological resilience monitoring framework to validate GAAN controllers in extended clinical trials. In ecology, true recovery from a disturbance is measured by tracking a suite of functional indicators over decades to plot a resilience trajectory. Adapted to this context, the long-term trial would be designed to measure a GAAN controller's efficacy by defining and monitoring a multi-dimensional 'functional recovery trajectory' for patients over years of therapy. This involves frequent, longitudinal assessment of a core set of complementary metrics (e.g., biomarkers, performance tasks, patient reports, and neuroimaging). The controller's success is then evaluated based on its ability to guide and sustain patient progress along an optimal recovery pathway, providing a dynamic, holistic measure of long-term therapeutic efficacy beyond a single endpoint.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Subtyping of Degenerative Disease via Disintegration of Learned Capability Hierarchies
ideas.description: This research constructs a computational framework that decomposes complex motor and cognitive functions into a hierarchy of sub-capabilities, informed by models of how these capabilities are acquired. It then uses longitudinal, multi-modal patient data to model how these sub-capabilities disintegrate over time. The core advance is the use of this structure to algorithmically identify distinct clinical subtypes of a disease based on their unique progression patterns through the capability hierarchy, linking each subtype to potential underlying pathological drivers. This enables more precise prognosis and the design of targeted therapeutic strategies.

ideas.title: Adapting Reservoir Computing's Echo State Networks for Efficient Patient Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that patient capability modeling requires complex, fully-trainable function approximators like Gaussian RBF networks. By drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and reservoir computing, specifically Echo State Networks, we propose a more efficient architecture. In that field, complex temporal systems are modeled using a fixed, random recurrent reservoir with only a simple linear readout layer trained, massively reducing computational cost. Adapted to this context, we would use a fixed, non-trainable reservoir (composed of simpler, non-Gaussian activation functions) to non-linearly project patient state and action history into a rich feature space. A computationally cheap, interpretable linear or shallow model is then solely responsible for learning the mapping from this fixed representation to patient capability, significantly lowering training cost and potentially improving robustness with fewer parameters.
