ideas.title: Provably Safe Hybrid Control for Nonlinear Manipulation via Embedded Linear Performance and Nonlinear Safety
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid control architecture that marries the performance and simplicity of linear methods with the guaranteed safety of nonlinear techniques for manipulation. The system employs a high-performance linear controller for nominal operation within a well-characterized region. A parallel supervisory module continuously verifies, using formal methods and online estimation, whether the system's trajectory will remain within safe, pre-computed bounds despite nonlinearities. If a safety boundary is threatened, the system seamlessly intervenes with a verified nonlinear safety filter or controller, ensuring robust stability and constraint satisfaction without sacrificing nominal performance.

ideas.description: This system learns assistive policies by first training a causal world model from historical patient interaction data to simulate realistic long-term trajectories. Instead of a fixed reward, an ensemble reward model is continuously trained via active learning from pairwise preferences provided by clinicians and patients on simulated policy behavior. Reinforcement learning is then performed within the simulated environment, using the learned reward model to guide policy optimization, with periodic real-world deployment of top policies for further preference collection and model refinement.

ideas.title: Multi-Model Adaptive Estimation with Online Model Falsification for Robust Patient Capability Tracking
ideas.description: This system robustly estimates patient capability by explicitly acknowledging and managing fundamental uncertainty in the underlying dynamical model. It runs a parallel bank of Kalman or particle filters, each instantiated with a different hypothesized model of capability change (e.g., linear recovery, asymptotic plateau, delayed decline). A supervisory algorithm computes the Bayesian posterior probability of each model being correct given the incoming stream of noisy measurements. If the data consistently invalidates all pre-specified models, the system alerts clinicians and can fall back to a conservative, less-assumptive estimation method. This approach provides reliable, uncertainty-quantified state estimates while guarding against the critical risk of model misspecification inherent in the original proposal.

ideas.title: Adaptive-Fidelity Hybrid Modeling with Complexity-Aware Gating for Patient Capability Assessment
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond a blanket simplification of the function approximator. It introduces a two-tiered, adaptive modeling system where a default low-cost model performs initial patient capability estimation. A gating mechanism, driven by a fidelity monitor evaluating prediction confidence and local data density, dynamically decides when to invoke higher-fidelity, more computationally intensive sub-models. This ensures computational resources are spent only on the complex, non-linear portions of the patient capability space that truly require it, guaranteeing model robustness and safety without sacrificing average-case efficiency.

ideas.title: Adaptive Learning Rate via Patient-Specific Bayesian Bandits
ideas.description: This idea personalizes the greedy strategy's learning rate in medical reinforcement learning by drawing an analogy from adaptive algorithms in computational finance. In algorithmic trading, systems use Bayesian bandit approaches (like Thompson Sampling) to personalize the learning or exploration rate for each financial asset based on its unique, dynamically updating volatility and momentum profile. The analogous solution is adapted by modeling each patient's recovery trajectory as a unique process with a latent 'responsiveness' parameter. A Bayesian framework is employed: a prior distribution over effective learning rates is set for a new patient, and this posterior is updated after observing the outcomes of each treatment decision. This allows the algorithm to automatically and continuously tailor its learning rate—controlling the exploration-exploitation trade-off—to the individual patient's observed recovery profile, leading to more personalized and efficient adaptive treatment strategies.
